This is done so that you can list large directories efficiently.

Wow, I hate the reason for it. But I understand the reasoning and it makes sense.

As devs using the Deno runtime, I suppose we can always polyfill `window` on `globalThis` or at the top-level for those times when the behavior is desirable on a per-project basis.

body:
For FormData, everything is currently stored in memory. In the future individual file blocks may be stored on disk. The amount of data stored will be capped just like in browsers, and exceeding those limits will cause formData to return a QuotaExceeded error.

It does seem reasonable that one may want to cap the total received length. Currently this can be done by intercepting the body stream and capping it in length. It would be much nicer if this could be done like this `req.formData({ maxByteLength: 4000 })`.

If that last option seems reasonable, I'll bring it up with the WHATWG for inclusion in the spec.
author: lucacasonato

body: Problem with `Content-Length` is that it is not reliable on HTTP/2. For HTTP/2, the content length header may be different to the actual length of the header.
author: lucacasonato

body:

That seems like an API that would be useful server-side, but that would not make too much sense client-side.

Of course, for Deno, `req.formData()` couldn't automatically download files into the filesystem before something like #10538 is implemented.

It's been my understanding that test files are run in parallel--they are not in the same global environment. In that case, your only other option is to have a "master" file that imports all other test files along with the polyfill, that is, if you are willing to sacrifice parallelism for it.

body:
If you are just wanting to use an existing npm package under Deno, the easiest way currently is to use a CDN to import from, like esm.sh or skypack.dev or esm.run.  While experimental, many npm packages can be run under Deno with the `--compat` mode flag without modification as well.

If you are looking to convert your existing npm package to Deno, including all the CI and scripts, there isn't anything specifically provided and supported by the core team, though we are continuing to explore this space.

To take a Deno "package" and export it to npm for consumption in Node.js, then there is [dnt](https://github.com/denoland/dnt).

body:
You simply need to delete `Deno.core` before you run any of the untrusted code.  For example:

**delete_core.js**

```js
delete Deno.core;
```

**main.js**

```js
import "./delete_core.js";

console.log(Deno.core); // undefined
```

All internal code binds early to `Deno` and `Deno.core` so deleting them won't impact downstream runtime code.

Regarding alternatives, there appears to be one in development: https://deno.land/x/deno_mongo_schema@v0.8.3

Mongoose, as far as I know, depends on Node's `crypto` module and although Deno has a Node compatibility mode, the support for Node's `crypto` is stated as 'partly' at this time: https://deno.land/manual@v1.20.1/node/compatibility_mode#node-built-in-modules. So it may not be possible to have Mongoose fully functional in Deno, it might require a port that rewrites it to use the WebCrypto API for example.

body:
> * `deno task build -- --dev` is more cumbersome to write than `./build.js --dev`

I recommend
```json
"tasks": {
    "build": "deno run --unstable --allow-write --allow-read --allow-run ./build.js",
    "build-dev": "deno run --unstable --allow-write --allow-read --allow-run ./build.js --dev"
}
```
rather than pretty much ever relying on `--`.

> Though I think what I would prefer the most is if `deno task` were able to parse `/usr/bin/env` shebangs somehow.

A lot of `deno task` is bridging Unix and Windows by re-implementing things for the latter. It wouldn't be out of place to supply a shebang parsing implementation for Windows, and a hook for `/usr/bin/env` as it has for some other commands already. @dsherret WDYT?
author: nayeemrmn

body:
> I recommend
> 
> ```json
> "tasks": {
>     "build": "deno run --unstable --allow-write --allow-read --allow-run ./build.js",
>     "build-dev": "deno run --unstable --allow-write --allow-read --allow-run ./build.js --dev"
> }
> ```
> 
> rather than pretty much ever relying on `--`.

Yeah this is what I'm using in most cases, but some scripts have so many arguments that it's infeasible to add all combinations. For instance my `test.js` script supports:

- `--coverage` to generate lcov coverage reports
- `--html` to also generate a html coverage report
- `--no-headless` to disable headless mode for puppeteer e2e tests
- `--inspect` and `--inspect-brk` to enable debugging the tests
- and on top of that it will pass along any unknown args over to `deno test` such as `--filter`

body:
I thought about having deno task support running a task like `./my_script.js`, but the downside is it encourages two ways of executing tasks for different sets of users. That said, if doing `"./my_script.js"` in a task already works on some platforms then perhaps we should just make it work on all. I opened https://github.com/denoland/deno_task_shell/issues/23

> Permissions are no longer in the same file as the main entry point.

Yeah, this is not as ideal, but I think there might be future improvements to the permission model that could solve this.

> But the downside is... it doesn't work very well on Windows.

I've heard it also doesn't work on some versions of Ubuntu.

> deno task build -- --dev is more cumbersome to write than ./build.js --dev

It should be possible to leave out the `--` and just do `deno task build --dev`. This is a command you can then recommend to people regardless of their operating system.

> Autocomplete for files no longer works

I opened #14039 to support shell completions for tasks in `deno task`.

body:
In singular cases (i.e. on main scripts) I do (assumming my script is main.ts and I wish to call it via `./cli` on Unix or `cli` on Windows):

```sh
ln -s main.ts cli
echo "@ECHO OFF" > cli.bat
echo 'deno run --allow-env --allow-read --allow-net --ext=ts main.ts "%@"' >> cli.bat
```

Yes, it produces more files, but a simple bat file can enable you Windows support without any hassle. I do it only for main scripts for my project. I keep using the shebang method for Unix based environments.

author: hironichu

body:
TLDR: Never put secrets in your code, this is a very bad practice. You should never version-control them either.

First, let me say that an executable file might look like gibberish when opened in a text editor, but it is actually a [structured file](https://jvns.ca/blog/2014/09/06/how-to-read-an-executable/).

Deno compile does for Deno what nexe or pkg do for Node: create a standalone, self-contained binary from your JavaScript or TypeScript source code, which means that your code is bundled as-is.

You could use obfuscation techniques to "hide" your code and secrets, but that just means it will be more difficult to see them, it won't be impossible. So you should not rely on that.

A great solution though would be to just **NOT** ship any kind of credentials with your code. Load them from a file, environment variables, etc. And never commit the file or else it will show in your repository history (even if you delete it afterwards).

author: dinogregorich

body:
It does not maintain a cache. It would a) be inefficient and b) break the model of the request being handled by the userland code.

The userland code would be significantly slower in a DDOS situation anyway, versus a per response compression.

If we were to implement response caching, it would align to the web platform APIs of `Cache` and `CacheStorage` and in those cases we could in theory sideload a compressed body in the response when a cached response was used. In those cases it would be up to the userland code to determine if the cached response is used or not anyway.
author: kitsonk

body:
I see; my only gripe is that compressing payloads every single time a resource is requested seems like a potentially colossal inefficiency, for some use cases.

For instance if a server is serving really big payloads (let's say 100MB+), it doesn't really make sense to compress that kind of payload a million times rather than one time.

But, this isn't the biggest deal because applications can always handle compression by itself, and automatic compression is still pretty handy for use cases where efficiency isn't of utmost importance.
author: cinolt

body:
> For instance if a server is serving really big payloads (let's say 100MB+), it doesn't really make sense to compress that kind of payload a million times rather than one time

Hopefully those wouldn't be sent as static bodies, but instead streamed, which currently is not compressed.

> But, this isn't the biggest deal because applications can always handle compression by itself, and automatic compression is still pretty handy for use cases where efficiency isn't of utmost importance.

"efficiency" is relative... serving huge static assets via a dynamic stack is inefficient, irrespective of them being compressed or not... trust me, if you are serving 100MB+ files with Deno, the compression in Rust wouldn't be the bottleneck.
author: kitsonk

body:
> "efficiency" is relative... serving huge static assets via a dynamic stack is inefficient, irrespective of them being compressed or not... trust me, if you are serving 100MB+ files with Deno, the compression in Rust wouldn't be the bottleneck.

Could you elaborate on what a "dynamic stack" is? My idea was to pre-allocate a 100MB+ Uint8Array then simply use that as a Response body each time. Seems like it would be pretty straightforward for Deno to send that as an HTTP response, so I don't know what's dynamic about that, unless Deno is doing something under the hood that would make that "inefficient"?
author: cinolt

body:
`deno -L` flag is for internal logging of Deno.  `console.*` is for _userland_ code.  We should make it clearer in the _help_ what logging it applies to.

`console.*` behaves a lot like it does in a browser. In a browser, `console.log()`, `console.info()`, `console.warn()`, `console.error()` change the "display" of the errors and advanced GUI allow filtering, but browsers do not allow "out of the box" suppression, nor does Node.js.  Node.js and Deno both send `console.error()` and `console.warn()` to stderr instead of stdout.

Adding out of the box suppression would be "surprising" to the established _de facto_ precedent established.

Because the _primitive_ logging is often not sufficient for a lot of workloads, people tend to use a logging framework.  There is a [logging framework](https://deno.land/std/log) as part of `std` library, but I think the larger community is split on its usefulness.  There are other [logging frameworks](https://deno.land/x/?query=log) available on `deno.land/x` that might be suitable as well.

If you are dealing with 3rd part code, then you might want to consider either patching the global `console` yourself, or use a package that helps you do that.

In the end, Deno treats `console.*` as a userland construct/API and doesn't _interfere_ with it.

author: bartlomieju

body:
Right now, `.debug()`, `.info()` and `.log()` send to `1` (stdout) while `.warn()` and `.error()` send to `2` (stderr), and while they make sense they are arbitrary.

Letting us specify an append-only destination for each level on the command line, being a file handle number or an actual file path, wouldn't do any suppression by itself but offer more flexibility. People could also choose to suppress by sending those logs to /dev/null. Invoked from Bash, this would even let you stream logs over the network directly (using Bash's [builtin network support](https://who23.github.io/2020/12/03/sockets-in-your-shell.html)).

@jespertheend thanks for opening this. As pointed out by Kit this is something I discussed with the rest of the team and want to improve to bring it closer to other popular tools like [vitest](https://vitest.dev/). 

The things I wanted to focus on in the first pass were:
- better visual separation of different test files (https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13930)
- improve final report, including failing tests details
- better handling of output from the the tests (console logs and direct writing to `Deno.stdout`/`Deno.stderr`)
- remove "internal" stack frames from errors (ie. don't show frames that code from Deno code)
- add "short" (or "dot" reporter)

In the second pass I want to use `miette` to provide nicer errors with source code context (this is a huge undertaking and requires severe changes to the test runner, but these changes are needed anyway to support timeouts in tests)

Thanks for the mockup, it's very useful. Feel free to open separate issues so we can discuss them more in depth.

author: lordanubi

body:
> The problem with the code you posted is that the cachedVersion function will return something like...

Yea, I'm not sure what your `cachedVersion()` function does under the hood. The idea is that you should try to avoid needing that `statSync()` call if possible due to the race condition I mentioned before. If this is code you control, you might be able to make sure that directory exists when you start the server.... but that's getting off topic.

> Any idea on how to use the async version in this situation?

Here are the docs for the asynchronous [`Deno.stat()`](https://doc.deno.land/deno/stable/~/Deno.stat). There is a code snippet there that shows how to call this function by `await`ing the returned `Promise`.

> I don't really know what it would be best to compromise performance wise

Synchronous code is usually a tiny bit faster than asynchronous code because the asynchronous code has more overhead and state to maintain. However, you'd be trading a small amount of latency for a big throughput hit. A general rule of thumb is that if you ever need to do multiple things at once in a performant way (like serve HTTP requests) then you should avoid synchronous code.

author: kedicesur

body:
> Thank you bartlomieju, in that case, what could be done then? If not, how to support large number of concurrent connections?

Is your workload bottlenecked by the HTTP handling itself, or the contained userland code that generates the responses? In the latter case, you could keep all the HTTP related stuff in the main thread, keep it lightweight, and only do the actual response generation in workers. So in the main thread you would generate a message object like `{action: 'dostuff', path: 'foo', data: 'bar'}`, then pass that to your worker pool. Worker would then respond with something like `{status: 200, type: 'text/html', body: 'lorem impsum'}`, and the HTTP handler in main thread would send a response based on that.

body:
This probably belongs in the [discussions](https://github.com/denoland/deno/discussions) section, as it's a question an not really a bug report / issue. That said i'll do my best to try to answer!
> Is deno ecosystem production ready now?

This depends on what your requirements are for production software, but Deno's been stable since v1.0, and there are plenty of [3rd party modules](https://deno.land/x) for things like db drivers, web frameworks, etc.

> Is there a git-ish registry like [cdnjs/cdnjs](https://github.com/cdnjs/cdnjs)?

Not sure what you mean by "git-ish", but Deno supports ES module imports, so you can host it on a cdn and import it via the url.

> Is there a cheat sheet, cover famous nodejs library's deno equivalents, or high lighted no equivalent, like koa to oak, listed in style like [rosetta](https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/Pacman/Rosetta)?

I don't think the Deno team has created anything like this, but if you search the third party deps, discussions & discord server, you could probably easily figure out if there's a Deno equivalent for any given node package. There's also a [compatibility layer](https://deno.land/manual/node) for running node projects in Deno.

> Is there a Hot-Module-Replacement guide?

There's `deno run --watch`, but I don't think there's hot module replacement. Looks like there's a tracking issue (#8327) for it though.

> Is there a way to alter partial dependency code, without effect other projects?

Not sure what you mean by this, sorry.

Hope this was helpful!

author: ghost

body:
> C/Unix veterans can quickly search up the right Deno function corresponding to the functionality they're used to

Couldn't you just as easily say people unfamiliar with the Unix terminology can look up the appropriate function calls for what they want? The Unix abstractions have been around for decades, and a lot of people are familiar with them. Often it makes sense to reuse abstractions instead of reinventing the wheel, because they'll pop up in other places too.

Some of the Unix calls do have pretty wacky names if you haven't used them before, but I think Deno does a pretty good job of documenting the api, and node.js (which is what all server side javascript devs have been using for years) uses a very similar api.
author: zachauten

body:
> Couldn't you just as easily say people unfamiliar with the Unix terminology can look up the appropriate function calls for what they want?

No, you couldn't say that, because the Unix pros already know the functionality they want, and just need to search for a single, likely unique, term (or simply infer that e.g. `fileInfo` probably uses a `stat` variant under the hood). People who don't know what the libraries are capable of instead need to browse the APIs by name. The entire point of intuitive or semantic names is that you can figure out what different API calls do very quickly, without having to read as much redundant documentation. You can explore in a REPL or with an autocomplete IDE much faster. If all you need is a unique hash table key, you may as well name everything with randomised ASCII characters, or do away with string names entirely and refer to every API function with a number. 

> The Unix abstractions have been around for decades, and a lot of people are familiar with them.

These are not abstractions we're discussing; these are better names for the exact same abstractions. Different concept entirely. 

> and a lot of people are familiar with them. 

A lot *more* people are familiar with their own native natural language, which is all you need to understand well-chosen names. Not only is that set of people much larger, but it's also a superset of the Unix-experienced set *and* the JS-fluent set. So, aim for the bigger set.

> Some of the Unix calls do have pretty wacky names if you haven't used them before

The names remain "wacky" (bad) for everyone else no matter how many times you, personally, use them. Deno doesn't document the rationale behind the break from naming conventions, as far as I've read, *and* that rationale would add to the aformentioned "redundant documentation" instead of just using a descriptive name.

> node.js (which is what all server side javascript devs have been using for years)

Deno already introduces massive breaking changes to Node users, and all of them are for the better. That's the entire point of Deno - a better / simpler / more consistent core design means it's faster to learn for *everyone* (meaning Node vets and everyone else, which *vastly* outnumbers Node vets), requires less repetition & boilerplate, is more practically useful for end-users, and has tonnes of other extremely worthwhile tangible benefits. You don't break APIs and habits for no reason; you break them to improve the state of the art.

I'm sorry for being rude, but I'd really rather you'd have thought about this rationally, instead of raising points I'd already covered in the OP and wasting everyone's time with a kneejerk response.
author: twome

body: The existing APIs cannot be removed, due to backwards compatibility. At best, they can be aliased with better, self-documenting names.
author: ghost

body:
> The existing APIs cannot be removed, due to backwards compatibility

[They can; that's what major versions are for.](https://semver.org/#how-should-i-handle-deprecating-functionality) You deprecate the old names and add the new names (which alias to the old) in a minor version, then when the time comes for the next major version, you fully replace the old names. Obviously this change alone would not be worth breaking compatibility for, which is why you bundle as many breaking changes as possible into a major version. You deprecate now, to give as much time as possible for people to switch existing projects to the future-proof API.

author: marcushultman

body:
I'm not part of the Deno team, but can give my view on a few things.

### FFI
Deno's FFI has recently been changing quite a bit, and moving to a more direct binding. The numbers used on Bun's website (around 350 ns per FFI call) seem in line with what the FFI performance was after my semi-recent addition of FFI callbacks, during which I also ended up adding a lot of JS-side overhead. A later PR by the core team has removed that overhead and further made the previously indirect FFI call binding a direct one, meaning that the current FFI performance is one order of magnitude better, around 33 ns per noop FFI call on my machine.

This will bring Deno's FFI more in line with Node's NAPI (33ns/op comes to about 29,600,00 ops per second) but falls a bit short, which is as expected since while Node's NAPI is not quite a direct FFI call API, it still gets to benefit from customized function bindings for each FFI function. This means that eg. a noop NAPI call doesn't need to check the passed in parameters (as I understand anyway), the bindings already know the parameters that the C function takes which is none in this case.

Bun's FFI is similar to Deno's in that it is bound from the JS side, instead of the JS side loading up ready-built bindings like Node does. What Bun does differently to Deno, however, is that it JIT-compiles a custom binding function for each FFI function call. This means that for the noop FFI call, Bun's binding layer drops down to nearly nothing, it's just the JS engine calling a C function which immediately turns around and calls the C FFI noop function, nothing more. Not even the return value needs to be handled.

This is a great way to lower the overhead of FFI calls. Deno currently has a draft PR open of implementing the same kind of system, so it might well be that Deno's FFI will soonish reach the same heights as Bun's does. With the V8 engine there's even a chance to go beyond what JavaScriptCore enables using the V8 Fast API Calls (though I may be wrong and maybe JSC also has something similar). Look forward to that.

### SQLite
While this benchmark is a fair benchmark in the sense of asking "how quickly can these different runtimes load a huge sqlite database", they're not exactly fair if the question is asked in a more limited fashion, eg. "using this given API, how quickly can these different runtimes load a huge sqlite database".

Bun has a big leg up here: They implement their own custom SQLite binding directly into the runtime, which gives them a great advantage in optimizing the performance of the binding. The Node package used, `better-sqlite3`, is a Node addon based library which then gets to utilize the performance of native code though dampened by the indirection that is the Node addon API.

Finally, the Deno library used is a WebAssembly based sqlite library. While WebAssembly is fast, it cannot quite measure up to pure native code. Of course between Deno and Wasm there is also a binding layer just how there is one between the others, so the Deno library doesn't get any magical wins here.

So, while the benchmark is definitely accurate for usage purposes, it's not really measuring the performance of Bun vs Node vs Deno but just different libraries doing the same thing in different runtimes.

### HTTP
With HTTP performance I have less understanding. I could point to Deno's ops (calls from JS code to code running on the Rust side) using a general purpose low-overhead binding layer whereas Bun I think may have a more hand-written binding layer between JS calls and the corresponding Zig functions. However, I think a big part of the reason for this performance result is simply that they've done a great job in writing the HTTP server in Bun.

I guess I'm also somewhat bound to note that there's an ongoing effort to push the HTTP server performance to even greater heights in Deno.
author: aapoalas

body:
The benchmark is not representative of many real world traffic scenarios. There are certain HTTP benchmarks where Deno is faster than Node, where Node will be faster than bun, or where bun will be fastest. You can not compare the performance of two systems through a single HTTP benchmark. The benchmark does not inspect request headers, request method, request url, or any number of other fields which are important in real traffic scenarios. It doesn't do routing, static file serving, etc.

Additionally these are just very different HTTP servers: Deno supports h2 and TLS on it's http server, while bun supports neither. We also can do automatic response body compression, full-duplex body streaming, etc.

Jarred is doing very interesting work with Bun, and that is pushing us to improve performance, but comparing system performance through a single isolated benchmark is not helpful.

If you are just looking for synthetic benchmarks where Deno is faster, [those](https://github.com/Jarred-Sumner/bun/issues/406) [exist](https://github.com/Jarred-Sumner/bun/issues/202). (They are not useful at all though).
author: lucacasonato

body:
I think you misread my comment. I never said that Bun can not do routing - I said that the referenced benchmark does not do any routing. There are however things that Deno's HTTP server does, that Bun does not:

- The Bun HTTP server does not send the HTTP `Date` header on responses. [This is a violation of HTTP/1.1 spec](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html#section-6.6.1) because it is an origin server with access to a reliable clock.
- Bun does not support HTTP/2.
- Bun does not support serving TLS encrypted HTTP traffic.

I stand by all statements in my original comment. They are all still true as of 14th October 2022.
author: lucacasonato

body:
I don't care about uWebSockets. I care about the API that Bun exposes, that I can access from JS. I am not comparing Hyper and uWebSockets. I am talking about Deno and Bun. And Bun does not support HTTP/2 or TLS termination, which is the only claim I made in my above comment.

And again, I never said that you can not do routing in Bun (or uWebSockets). I said that the linked benchmark does not do routing, and because of that (and other things), I don't think it is a good benchmark. Please read my original comment...

"The Bun Http server" is uWebSockets. That's my project so I wanted to correct some misunderstandings.

There's always radix-tree routing performed, even if you don't see it. This includes wildcards and parameter matching. None of that is bypassed in bun (even if bun.serve doesn't expose it).
All headers are lowercased and put in a bloom filter so even if you don't access them they've already been processed and have O(1) access.

TLS 1.3 is perfectly supported and very fast
You definitely can stream large responses both in and out - that's not something deno does exclusively which you seem to imply by bringing it up.

body:
it's like a global variable for a unique call stack.
Image in the javascript code there is a function called `handleRequest(req)` which handles HTTP requests.
On the rust side, we start a web server using something like `warp` or `hyper` crates. basically receive the request from `warp` and pass it to the `handleRequest(req)` by calling it. We need a unique id for each request and we don't want to explicitly pass it down via function parameters like `handleRequest(req, request_id)`. There are some custom implementations of functions like `fetch` that can call something like `getRequestId()` without passing anything to it and receiving the request's id.
Basically scoped variables (thread local variables) for async functions.

body:
Deno doesn't support service workers.  You mean web workers?

Really that is the question for the framework authors.

Hono isn't specifically a Deno framework, so there are the considerations of other platforms.  Typically cloud based workers don't support additional "owned" workers and achieve scalability by multiple instances, so it might not ever make sense for something like Hono.

Ultimately, web workers have a lot of limited usability, where you are doing a lot of processing but there is little data transfer between the main worker and the web worker, especially when it comes to processing network requests, there is almost 0 benefit, because the runtime is really only handling requests with a single worker in mind.  In most cases you really want to look at making the main thread as performant as possible, blocking as little as possible.

author: reggi

body:
Question is a bit confusing because deno is purely server side. But you talk about requests which are a client side concern. I think the best approach for organizing deno/serverside code is clear. Client/browser side code delivery can be a different animal.  

Node and other historical platforms have steered us to create all kinds of over-engineered solutions and magic; we forget that with modern javascript and deno, we can forget all that and keep things very simple.

- For server side deno code, forget all build steps, module servers and other contraptions. The standard, built in ES way of import/export is all you need. Just use separate files everywhere where it makes sense for readability of code and separation of loosely related functionalities. Deno takes care of caching and loading everything efficiently. When everything is organized neatly to small modules, you get pretty good tree-shaking for free, as any module you use (import directly), will only load the dependencies it needs, all the way down the dependency graph. You should author your libraries so that users can import modules for the specific functionalities they need, instead of importing huge all-inclusive module that contain all of the project. This is easy with standard ES import/export stuff.

- For non-deno, client side browser code, arguably the same approach can work well, if you serve invidual files with proper caching headers and tuning other aspects of how you serve files. If your client side app is starting to get slow, you can look into some bundling or building solutions that can take a dependency graph and create a monolithic file out of that. But that only if/when the standard approach starts to cause problems and tuning your server and caching isn't enough.

- For code that is common to both deno/server and the client/browser, the same approach works, as long as you have proper entrypoint modules, and use fine grained dependencies/separation of code, so that client is not dragging server-specific dependencies, and vice versa.

body:
eg. This will work:
```bash
> deno info https://deno.land/x/oak
Download https://deno.land/x/oak
Warning Implicitly using latest version (v11.1.0) for https://deno.land/x/oak
Download https://deno.land/x/oak@v11.1.0
error: module could not be found

Note the error is about not finding a module with no name. If I add `/mod.ts`  it will show me a lot more stuff (as `info` command shows the dependency graph) but also, it will not necessarily show me the latest but will instead show what I have in cache, or if none then it will implicitly use the latest version.

> deno info https://deno.land/x/oak/mod.ts

type: TypeScript
dependencies: 76 unique (total 784.95KB)

https://deno.land/x/oak@v7.3.0/mod.ts (1.84KB)
├─┬ https://deno.land/x/oak@v7.3.0/application.ts (17.23KB)
│ ├─┬ https://deno.land/x/oak@v7.3.0/context.ts (6.8KB)
│ │ ├── https://deno.land/x/oak@v7.3.0/application.ts *

You can also add the `--reload` flag to the above command to force Deno to clear the old version from cache and fetch the new version.

body:
The biggest challenge with any architecture is not specifically the availability of the hardware but the challenge of integrating that hardware into the CI pipeline which is currently GitHub Actions based.

In addition a lot of core dependencies would need be ported over to RISC-V to make it work.  It does appear for V8 the [changes have been merged](https://groups.google.com/a/groups.riscv.org/g/sw-dev/c/jQi2yIq7jaA) but there can be a lot of pitfalls and the like with the other tooling to make it smooth.  I believe for other architectures we have accepted patches into [rust_v8](https://github.com/denoland/rusty_v8), but there are still [~540 dependencies](https://deno.land/benchmarks#cargo-deps) that would need be cleanly built against a RISC-V target.

Several of the core team are huge fans of the RISC-V architecture. That being said, would you rather want the core team working on porting Deno to an interesting but still currently hobbyist architecture, or fixing bugs and delivering features that would benefit 99% of the users?  For now, it feels like it would need to be a community effort to get something that builds on RISC-V and likely, if there are changes that need to be merged upstream, they would likely be accepted as PRs.

body:
I will admit this _does_ get into the ugly world of file loaders and general asset management, but I think thats exactly what we need here, management of resources required to run a deno app. The advantage to putting this behind some kind of ffi specific flag in the import specifier is that we can at least restrict downloading random files to when `--allow-ffi` is enabled. The specific protocol matters less to me. Any of these seem fine:
```ts
import { path } from 'https://deno.land/x/native/sqlite3?type=ffi'
import { path } from 'ffi:https://deno.land/x/native/sqlite3'
import { path } from 'ffi!https://deno.land/x/native/sqlite3'

author: johndeighan

body:
I just found the following on this page: https://docs.deno.com/runtime/reference/cli/task_runner/

Unlike in most shells, the first async command to fail will cause all the other commands to fail immediately. In the example above, this would mean that if the client command fails then the server command will also fail and exit. You can opt out of this behavior by adding || true to the end of a command, which will force a 0 exit code.

So, if you do this:

task 1 & task 2 & task 3 & task 4

Tasks 1, 2 and 3 will run in the background, task 4 will run in the foreground. The text above implies that if any of tasks 1, 2 or 3 fail, the other 2 will immediately fail. That's great, but I think it does NOT mean that if task 4 fails (e.g. by entering Ctrl-C), that the first 3 tasks will also fail. 

Still, maybe it means that if the original example at the top of this page ran "deno task foo & deno task bar &", it would achieve the desired result, i.e. run 2 tasks in parallel in such a way that if one failed, the other would also terminate (note the trailing &)
author: johndeighan

body:
I don't think think it works like that, but maybe I am misunderstanding what you are saying.  [Here](https://github.com/konsumer/deno-tasks-example) is an example. If you Ctrl-C before the 5s timeout, both sub-tasks exit.

```
Task p deno task a & deno task b
Task b deno b.js
Task a deno a.js
b: Hi!
a: Hi!
^C
```

If you let them both run:

```
Task p deno task a & deno task b
Task b deno b.js
Task a deno a.js
a: Hi!
b: Hi!
before goodbye
before goodbye
a: Bye!
b: Bye!
```

Here, a will run, and b, and both exit on Ctrl-C.

If I run the same thing in a normal shell (I am using zsh here) it backgrounds a, then starts b, and Ctrl-C only effects b:

```
deno task a & deno task b
[1] 240231
Task b deno b.js
Task a deno a.js
a: Hi!
b: Hi!
^C
❯  before goodbye
a: Bye!

[1]  + 240231 done       deno task a
```

> Hi there,
>

> TL;DR Questions
>

>    company)

> Use-Case
>
> I work at a company behind a firewall (as many people).
>
> I would like to use deno as a lightweight scripting environment to avoid
> long-running npm installs in ci.
>
> I also would like to use deno'a fresh package for lightweight status pages
> about our running services.
>
> I'm able to install deno on my Developer machine, since it doesn't need

> server has no real internet connection.
>

> We have an openshift kubernetes cluster, which contains the Jenkins
> server. Each build node for this Jenkins is an ephemeral container which
> gets started for each individual build und then discarded.
> Note: I can't add a new base image for build nodes, due to permissions.

>

>

>    be installed with every build run.
>

>

>    - or I have to Setup a small deno.deploy service, which regularly
>    checks the version of the deno executable at the normal download endpoint.
>

> 2. When a new deno binary is out, this event should trigger a GitHub

>
>    if we cannot do that, do we release one deno package per system and
>    arch and add that to the naming of the package?

>

body:
> *Lately I think about using Rust for my personal life, I'm thinking about installing redox-os which is written in rust and has some basic applications.*

For what it's worth, I wouldn't say that you are really using Rust by using OSes/applications that are written in Rust, like I wouldn't say that a non-programmer is using any particular programming language by using an OS.

> *My idea would be to have the deno inside redox-os so I can create webapps, pwa(progressive-web-application), spa(single-page-application), mpa(multi-page-application) and native stuff with Rust through the cargo dependency manager.*

You can't really create web apps with just Deno itself - that's done by libraries, website front-ends, etc., Deno is just a serverside JavaScript runtime, but those are not done in Rust, from Deno.

> *I'm really enjoying things like Rust, redox them, deno and cargo. If I could put all these tools together, part of my life would be happier as a developer.*

Unless I'm mistaken, Redox is an OS? If so, you can't redox applications, such as Deno, nor will you really be using Rust from Deno, modulo loading FFI dynamic libraries which could've been compiled from Rust. But if the goal is to be writing web applications in Rust, then it sounds like Deno wouldn't suit your goals well.

How interesting that you link that @steida , because by definition: "The dependency issue arises when several packages have dependencies on the same shared packages or libraries, but they depend on different and incompatible versions of the shared packages"

author: Soremwar

body:
There's no need to be sarcastic here. There's no silver bullet in dependency management, that's obvious, but import maps simply aren't satisfying. They require configuration for the library consumer. This is unacceptable. It has to be possible to copy-paste-run the code from my library's README. Explaining the issue of peer dependencies there and forcing users to create a JSON configuration file because of some internals is far from ideal.

I've said it in #10356 already, I'll repeat it here: Node can do it. (Yes, that means Bun can do it, too.) I wish we could come up with a solution for this for Deno. So far, this seems to be a pending task.

Our current solution for our ~30 plugins that support Deno is to rely on https://lib.deno.dev. This will redirect to the latest version. That way, each plugin automatically imports the latest version of the core library. That aligns perfectly with what users import in an ideal world. In practice, this breaks every once in a while, but people can fix it easily using `--reload`. This is becoming the Deno equivalent of `rm -rf node_modules && npm i`, which clearly isn't nice, but it's the least bad solution we have. It certainly works better for us than import maps do.

Perhaps someone can come up with something better, I'd be glad to hear about it.
author: KnorpelSenf

body:
I'm not trying to be sarcastic, but I'm sorry if I came out as too blunt

What I'm trying to say is that in this ecosystem where peer dependencies aren't widely available and every library mostly pings to a specific version we kinda sidestepped the dependency hell problem. Yes we will find some level of library duplication the more we dig into our dependency tree but at no point we'll find ourselves with incompatible versions of a library as we are bound to do in another ecosystem such as Node/NPM

I understand it has its drawbacks, but the more I use it the more convinced I am that if we delegate dedupping subdependencies to us (the end users) we'll find ourselves in less dependency hell situations. I understand that this means that the end user will always have to do dedupping manually, but in my opinion it's a better compromise than a project that can't be updated 1 year in the future out of fear of dependencies incompatibility
author: Soremwar

body:
That sounds great in theory and I actually believed the same thing in the beginning. In practice, we have some non-trivial type errors that result from users composing various elements from the plugins and the core library. They're incompatible (on the type level) if they stem from different minor versions.

We're already doing a fair bit of black TypeScript magic to avoid most of this, and sometimes we're able to cast types internally to make sure the errors go away, but this only gets us so far. Until then, people will have to `--reload` to do the manual deduping you're talking about.

In a way, peer deps could be seen as DI on the module level. I think it could be possible to move this to the code level via dynamic imports. I'm honestly not sure if that's any better due to the drawbacks of dynamic imports, what do you think?
author: KnorpelSenf

body:
> That sounds great in theory and I actually believed the same thing in the beginning. In practice, we have some non-trivial type errors that result from users composing various elements from the plugins and the core library. They're incompatible (on the type level) if they stem from different minor versions

I really don't understand what you mean by this. Typechecking will not be affected by importing different versions of the same library

> People will have to --reload to do the manual deduping you're talking about

`--reload` will do nothing for a pinned version

I'm kinda confused by what you describe here, could you elaborate @KnorpelSenf ?
author: Soremwar

body:
> > That sounds great in theory and I actually believed the same thing in the beginning. In practice, we have some non-trivial type errors that result from users composing various elements from the plugins and the core library. They're incompatible (on the type level) if they stem from different minor versions
> 
> I really don't understand what you mean by this. Typechecking will not be affected by importing different versions of the same library

Why not? In our case, users import a value (some deeply nested object which allows users to perform API calls) from one version, and a function consuming that object from another version, but if the API gets extended, a version mismatch will lead to compilation errors, even though no runtime code will fail.

> > People will have to --reload to do the manual deduping you're talking about
> 
> `--reload` will do nothing for a pinned version

As mentioned before, we use `lib.deno.dev` in order to update versions automatically through redirects, rather than pinning versions.

An alternative to our solution would be up ship dozens of patch updates for every release. Every plugin would have to be updated and the only change would be an updated dependency on the core lib.
author: KnorpelSenf

body:
> In a way, peer deps could be seen as DI on the module level. I think it could be possible to move this to the code level via dynamic imports. I'm honestly not sure if that's any better due to the drawbacks of dynamic imports, what do you think?

By the way, this will not work well because Deno Deploy does not support dynamic imports—but we obviously want to be able to use the library there. So this is not a solution, either.

body:
I think [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CPU_time) provides a sufficient definition. It means that time spent waiting on a network request would not be billed.
> CPU time (or process time) is the amount of time for which a central processing unit (CPU) was used for processing instructions of a computer program or operating system...
...
> In contrast, elapsed real time (or simply real time, or [wall-clock time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wall-clock_time)) is the time taken from the start of a computer program until the end as measured by an ordinary clock.

body: My guess is that this rule is related to the `this` value of arrow functions. The guide says "Arrow syntax should be limited to closures." Functions often need to have a `this`, while closures generally just need access to the closed over variables.

body: This type of thing is the first thing that comes to mind: https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/blob/6767ad853f0e0cead74281220f00aaf1efa34455/node/_fs/_fs_streams.ts#L234-L235
author: cjihrig

body: Thank you for sharing your insights. Most team members I've worked with in the past (and myself too) generally agree with everything you've said which is why I was surprised to see Deno's internal style guide specify not using arrow functions on the top-level. I'm confident there are reasons but there's no current explanation as to why in the guide which prompted me to reach out and ask. (Maybe it has something to do with Rust idioms or something else specific to the stack the Deno team works with that makes it work better for them.)
author: mfulton26

body:
> (Maybe it has something to do with Rust idioms or something else specific to the stack the Deno team works with that makes it work better for them.)

Well, (although a member of their team still hasn't responded) this sounds plausible: Rust generally uses functions `fn f(x, y, x) { w }` at the top-level, and closures `let f = |x, y, z| w` elsewhere.

body:
@mechanoid Thanks for the suggestions!

`--grant-env=XYZ` looks interesting to me. What happens if `--grant-env=DEBUG` is specified but `--allow-env` is not specified? Does it make sense to make `--grant-env=DEBUG` implying `--allow-env` implicitly?

> Especially with the npm world becoming more accessible, the deno permission spirit faces lots of libraries written in a different mindset, eagerly reading all env variables without hesitation.

Right, we also observe that a lot of npm modules access a lot of env vars even when they are not necessary for them to work correctly.

Currently we also consider adding options like `Deno.env.get("FOO", { prompt: false })` / `Deno.env.toObject({ prompt: false })` which don't ask permissions to users, but throws instead (and we can use them in getter of `process.env`)
author: kt3k

body:
Hey,

If  `--allow-env` is not specified at all one can set `--allow-env={GRANTED VARS}` implicitly, and I guess that would be a better UX as letting people define the same thing twice 👍 

The prompt option seems also a really good idea, but again it leaves people without possibility to use those packages that ask for too much. I think a combination of both would be great though!

Thanks a lot for your feedback, and happy to be able to contribute!
author: mechanoid

body: Hi @kt3k , @bartlomieju , sorry to interrupt you and approach you this directly. I think this topic is rather critical, as the adoption of `npm` is increasing. Is there a better way to address/track this, as a discussion? Thx and best regards!
author: mechanoid
kt3k

body:
hey @kt3k, thanks for your quick response! 

But I think that this does not address the same problem. This would allow to check if you can access an Env Var at runtime, but I think the major problem is, that there is a huge deal of npm packages out there, that are overly obsessive about reading env variables. As long as you need to rely on such dependency you will end up with a generic `--allow-env` runtime permisson, 
effectively disabling env-var checks for your complete program.
author: mechanoid

body:
In a bit of a similar fashion, I'm running into this issue with `firebase-auth`. Like many Node libraries, it does some arbitrary checks to `process.env`. I don't want to give it access to those variables. However, I am fine with it just erroring silently. For example, `process.env.HOME` etc could just return `undefined` for the library.

This is common as libraries often do `if (process.env.SOME_VAR) doThis()`. And I'd like to be able to use those libraries without enabling the env variables they want necessarily.

author: justinmchase

body:
In addition to @zachauten 's suggestions the only other approach I see so far is here:
https://deno.land/x/fake_imports@v0.6.1

Where it basically pre-resolves all of the imports, loads the files into memory, rewrites all of the imports and generates temporary in memory Blob urls with certain modules overwritten with your mock code and then resolves that instead. It even seems to cleverly generate source maps so all the urls are relative to the original files as well as respecting your actual import maps.

Also, I found this ecmascript proposal which may be taking into consideration such scenarios:
https://github.com/tc39/proposal-import-assertions/issues/46

Ran into the same issue (also Windows 10) and found this discussion. Definetely not an issue with Deno, but I'll leave a solution that helped me here in case someone also finds this.

The solution was to change the port. To fix the two code examples in the first post you should change `port: 3000` and `port: 8008` to, for example, `port: 10001`.

In my case I was looking at Fresh. I created a new app (`deno run -Ar jsr:@fresh/init`), then when I ran `deno tash dev` I encountered the same error. In my case adding `server: { port: 10001 }` to `vite.config.ts` fixed the problem.

author: bartlomieju

body:
(copying my earlier response from the initial issue to this discussion)

There's no question about it; for Deno, it is better to use a library specifically made for Deno. It will use modern, mostly standard APIs Deno is built around, so they likely have cleaner, more efficient codebase, and more "modern" API. Also the additional complexity of node compatibility layer is avoided.

However, like with any library, one needs to be mindfull of how well any given library is maintained and supported. Node has been around for a long time, and is very popular. Thus many npm libraries are more mature compared to the relatively young Deno libraries that may have some bugs, missing features, and smaller community of forward-looking developers doing the legwork of ironing all the small issues out.

So, I suggest choose the Deno library by default. If that is incomplete or doesn't work out for you for some reason, only then fall back to the NPM version.

body:
I think your reference point you're using to judge the success of Deno is a bit faulty. Svelte, for example, was initially released in 2016 which was a good two years before Deno.

Node.js has traditionally been the main runtime for JS and TS tooling which is why it's nearly universal. It has a much bigger user base and market share so a small team working on a new idea is going to want to maximize the return on their efforts by targeting them over other options like Deno or Bun. 

The Deno team has made impressive strides to provide compatibility with the npm and node environment. They also, in my opinion, provide a much nicer DX so I believe we'll see adoption continue to grow. There is a LOT to learn here but funny enough, Deno helps to take care of a decent amount of that overhead (like trying to setup a typescript project in Node.js!) and lets you really jump into coding.

I do agree that it still has some issues with providing simpler plug and play solutions or configurations for generating web UI using the more popular frameworks, but those frameworks are __complicated__ and there's a lot involved with making it work.
author: MrPossumz

body:
For me, with no prior JS stack experience, Deno was a no brainer but I think I must have turned up just as those "strides" had been taken. I read the docs fully before starting out and it seemed that interop with the Node world was probably going to be fine.

So I've been surprised that the frameworks, especially the newer CSR+SSR ones that I'd imagine people would be looking to use on greenfield projects, have not got Deno and Node as equal weighted choices from the start. My guess is that maybe the knowledge of the npm compatibility work hasn't permeated yet and the people working on these frameworks need a little nudge to get them to look again at first class Deno support from the get-go.

I eventually started down the Fresh route, though I'm not sure if that's a "serious" framework or a demonstration. I watched a session at a Remix event with Ryan Dahl announcing it, and he seemed to act like it wasn't a serious thing. He may just have been playing it down given that he'd apparently come to a Remix event to shill his own framework but I don't know. It's all too new to me.

TS working without any funny business, the VSCode extension works a treat (including showing available tests to run, great!), even the module preference over packages has been a breathe of fresh air. I think what's missing is developers just building stuff with Deno, better docs, and fixing bugs. NPM compat is huge, now there's the final stretch of getting it to work seamlessly with Deno (e.g npm based bundlers!).

author: bartlomieju

body:
> Is it the recommended way to import a _npm_ module as a library author? If I understand correctly, importing a dependency in _deno_ does not take the dependency's import map into account?

That's correct, library authors are not meant to use import maps - they do not "cascade" or "stack", only the final import map is applied.

body:
In Deno, Go-style streaming (`Deno.reader/writer`) used to be the norm. However, the Web Stream API was later implemented instead of Go-style streaming, and it is now recommended to use it.

ref: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/9795#issuecomment-874347722, https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/issues/1986

`readableStreamFromReader` and `writableStreamFromWriter` are utilities provided (to my understanding) to migrate from Go-style streaming to Web Stream API.
Therefore, I think it is recommended to write in the second style (using only Web Stream API) unless there is a special reason.
author: ayame113

body:
yes, using the getter is the recommended way. the former way is just legacy by now.

additionally, to answer the question:
> Is there a reason for that?

the main one being is the former way was around for quite a while longer, unlike the streams properties which were added quite later

body:
These attributes are just a metric, not something functionally effective. there are currently 3 attributes:

- popularity metric: shows on a star-based rating the popularity of a module. 1 star means its in the top 10%, 2 stars in the top 5% and 3 stars in the top 1%.
- "By Deno Team": means the module is officially maintained by the deno team (meaning its under the denoland org)
- "comes with Deno config": means the module uses a deno config file. this is just a quality metric

body:
What is the point of offering an API to implement gRPC clients and servers, rather than directly supporting gRPC clients and servers via the Deno CLI?
One interest I can see is to simplify the support of protocols like [Connect](https://connect.build/) or gRPC-Web via a package, but I don't know if it is enough to justify the complexity? (Especially since it would be ideal to support them directly in Deno.)
author: ghost

body:
I mean, implementing gRPC with TypeScript on Deno is too hard (or impossible to implement properly). The reason why is above description.
Implementing [Connect](https://connect.build/) or [gRPC-Web](https://github.com/grpc/grpc/blob/master/doc/PROTOCOL-WEB.md) properly using `fetch` and `Deno.serveHttp` is possible and unlikely to degrade performance, so I think it's pointless to add them to the Deno runtime API. However, it might be worth considering going into the Deno standard library.

My company is already actively doing MSA with gRPC, and we considered making an API gateway server using Deno.
Since it is impossible to support gRPC in Deno right now, we are writing the gateway server on Node.js, but since Deno is a much safer (even faster) and more convenient development environment, we want to switch to this if possible.

It is a very difficult task for us to replace gRPC with other protocols such as Connect or gRPC-Web. This is because we need to add Connect or gRPC-Web support for the various languages used by each microservice.
author: disjukr

body: @johynpapin What do you mean by "directly supporting gRPC clients and servers via the Deno CLI"? Does that mean you want a somewhat higher level of API to be provided? If that's the case, the original post includes the reason why the API is proposed with lower level in mind.

body:
If we could just find a way to just expose the missing bits of the HTTP2 spec to support more advanced uses like a GRPC implementation that would be perfect.  This would probably be more useful in general than implementing and supporting GRPC itself as part of the runtime API.

This could mean exposing a lower-level HTTP API that could, for example, be used to implement the existing `fetch`-based HTTP server.  The existing `fetch` stuff already has some "inner" entities as part of its implementation, perhaps some of this could be exposed in a more elegant way with this sort of missing functionality.
author: reyawn
ghost

body:
Hi, I just took a brief excursion thru hitting firestore.googleapis.com gRPCs from Deno :) (I've ported another nodejs http2 impl up to w3c streams etc. to try doing a more serious take on user-code gRPC)

> @reyawn From what I understand, the fetch API, following old discussions, does not allow to retrieve trailers. But if I'm not mistaken, this is the only thing missing.

Trailers definitely look like the big missing piece in `fetch()`, and yea, no apparent plans to spec it. You cannot receive gRPC errors at all without trailers! if you try sending a gRPC thru fetch today you will likely get back an empty `200 OK` and literally nothing else. (Similarly, serving gRPC requires the ability to _send_ trailers)

But also grpc wants to receive the HTTP2 RST_STREAM code (stream reset reason, when the server must abandon a request) in order to translate it into grpc error codes like `INTERNAL` or `UNAVAILABLE`. [The spec has a table](https://github.com/grpc/grpc/blob/master/doc/PROTOCOL-HTTP2.md#errors). Not critical to have, but sounds important IMO to offer spec-complete gRPC.

So, trailers and RST_STREAM visibility seem like the needed bits to satisfy the spec. But just to unnerve a little, grpc-web proxy spec says that grpc HTTP/2 wants to be aware of HTTP/2 framing, and that browser APIs will never expose that framing:

> For the gRPC-Web protocol, we have decided on the following design goals:
> [...] decouple from HTTP/2 framing which is not, and will never be, directly exposed by browsers [...]
author: danopia

body: Thank you very much for these details! I didn't know about `RST_STREAM`. :pray:
author: ghost

body: I'm currently working on modernizing our HTTP implementation to live on top of Hyper 1.0 and refactoring this code pretty significantly. gRPC will be next on my list, so I'll make sure that we're in a good place to support trailers/etc, at least on the `Deno.serve` side.
author: mmastrac

body: @mmastrac That's fantastic.  Ultimately it would be ideal for new libraries with great backing and momentum like https://github.com/bufbuild/connect-es to be able to utilize this functionality in a similar mechanism to other integrations (fastify, nodejs vanilla http2 server, etc)!  `connect-es` has minimal dependencies (e.g. nothing like the old, non-spec-compliant, and troublesome-to-use `grpc-js`, `protobufjs`, etc libraries in the ecosystem), fully implements GRPC, and best of all is fully ESM-compliant so should be a great fit for Deno.

Note that `postMessage` uses `structuredClone algorithm` [1] and `Request` type is not listed as a supported type [2] to be serialized by that algorithm. I don't really know the details about this subject, but I suppose that this is why your code didn't work.

[1] https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Window/postMessage#parameters
[2] https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Web_Workers_API/Structured_clone_algorithm#webapi_types
author: augustollenz

body: That is the answer, but also, Deno doesn't currently support structured cloning of any web APIs. The issue is #12067, and adding support for this turned out to be much trickier than expected because of the complexity of adding a new `rusty_v8` scope (denoland/rusty_v8#862).

body:
Sometimes a simple folder with stuff inside might be enough, as @jtoppine mentioned, but in other situations you might have more complex use cases.

I couple months ago I stumbled across the same limitation and found this thread, but found no suitable tools for this issue, so I decided to create my own Deno monorepo manager.

## [Compy - Minimalist (yet helpful) monorepo manager for Deno](https://deno.land/x/compy)

https://deno.land/x/compy

> **Disclaimer:** Compy is still in early stage and some features might be redesigned in future versions. Automated testing and proper documentation is also in progress.

That said, it does provides good tooling for creating a project and its modules, adding dependencies and mapping them through import maps, running Deno's CLI commands within a module's context and linking modules between themselves. In fact, I'm currently using it in production.

Some commands include:
- `compy init <project>` to create a new project/workspace
- `compy mod <module>` to create a new module within the project
- `compy add std` to add the latest std version to all modules
- `compy add oak v12.6.1 -m <module>` to add `oak@v12.6.1` to an specific module
- `compy <cache|start|dev|run|test|fmt|lint> <module>` to run a command within a module's context
- and more

Here's a simple (outdated) demo:
![Untitled-2xc](https://github.com/denoland/deno/assets/1886960/e89d0c96-3a2a-43f7-98c7-d1d987554c55)

@bradenmacdonald @carere you might find this helpful as well.

body:
I'm suprised you get "Hello World" printed at all. `deno_core` has only access to the `console` object provided by V8, which by default doesn't print at all (though it redirects the logs to the inspector if it is connected).

The `undefined` is printed because the value returned from evaluation of `console.log()` is `undefined`.

If you want to have more control over console, you should use `deno_console` crate, you can then create a new `console` instance like this:
```
globalThis.console = new console.Console((msg, level) => Deno.core.print(msg, level > 1));
```

The argument that the constructor accepts is a "print" function that in the case above uses built-in `Deno.core.print` function to output to stdout/stderr.

body:
I'm surprised nobody has engaged with this comment. The inability to use jest-style module mocking is baffling to me. I've already spent way more time trying to figure out how to do this very simple thing, than setting up a full testing flow in Node + jest would have taken.

And IF this functionality exists, then its documentation must be poor or non-existent because no search, ChatGPT, nor any other system has been able to find any reference to Deno's ability to do this. You mean the @std/testing/mock library? Yeah it doesn't mock an import by replacing it. If I want to do that, I have to use a custom import map and re-create the entire import surface of my project, which is quite large. All the library does is give me the tools to completely rebuild every library I import.
author: codinronan

body: There is currently no way to mock globals or modules in Deno's own test runner. If your tests rely on that feature then you're better served with `jest` or `vitest` for the time being.

author: woifes

body: No, it's not planned at the moment as we're working on other areas of Deno at the moment. It's best to stay with `jest` or `vitest` if mocking is a requirement.

body: Today I fooled around with a small test project. Trying to get jest running with deno. Unfortunately I was not successfull. There is also no real guide I could find somewhere. Especially with module mocking which is digging itself very deeply into the module loading system of (normally) node I strongly doubt that this is even possible. And in the end if I migrate to deno I do it to have less work in setting up the tooling instead of even more. I still keep an eye on deno but is is still no alternative to me :(

Mine would be that I lift all unit test which would require mocking of 3rd party components up to a integration test level. On the one hand it would ofc rise the test quality at one end but on the other hand I doubt that I will be able to achive the same "depth" in testing. And ofc the effort and footprint of the tests will also rise.

body: Thanks for bumping this! I come from .NET world where I always had to design my architecture in a way that allows to test the code in future (setup IoC containers etc.). This is huge overhead. When I start some new project I prefer much more to focus on "making it happen", iterate quickly and when final shape is ready I would like to focus on testing. Ability to pick up any file and test it thoroughly by mocking all global variables/imports would be an amazing feature and IMHO perfect selling point to convince people to start using Deno.

I'm in much the same position, most of my JS career's testing has been based around the same idea you describe and I think it'd be a great set of features in Deno - it would also allow the popular testing libraries to write Deno ports, which would increase the size of the ecosystem.

For now I think I'm going to have to fallback to jest and simply replace my Deno-specific imports (mostly @std/lib stuff) on a case by case basis. Still more time than I'd like it to have taken, but a solution that will work for now.

Later edit: No way to get Jest to understand deno-style package management (you can get npm to install jsr, but can't get jest to use them). Will be falling back to deno and going with the "replace packages with mocks via import_map" approach.

Having said that... the frontend world loves importing no-js modules to get a dynamically generated url path.  I'm just not sure what the best way is to work around that on the frontend other than using jest or vitest.

In my opinion, testing is a big part of better engineering in many settings, and I think import mocks are basically necessary to do any significant amount of JS testing.  Also, one thing that LLM's are really good at is writing specs, so I think we are also seeing the cost/benefit of writing tests improve substantially.  Many existing Node based projects also rely heavily on import mocking, and not having the feature available in Deno is going to block migration.

body:
Your code is almost fine - replace:
```
    let str = res.open(runtime.v8_isolate()).to_rust_string_lossy(&mut runtime.handle_scope());
```
with:
```
    let str = res.open(&mut scope).to_rust_string_lossy(&mut scope);
```
and it should work.

Since you already opened one handle scope there's really no point in this situation to create another. And for `res.open()` call you can also use existing `HandleScope` (because it internally defers to `Isolate`).

author: rwilcox

body:
I empathize with the Deno team. Creating a competitive runtime with backwards compatibility with Node is a challenging task. Think about it: Deno has implemented web standards through and through and defaults to what browsers do, as seen in things like URL imports, fetch, request, response, etc. In contrast the world of modern web development is pretty non-standard. Pretty much all modern frameworks create their own flavor of JavaScript (specialized module exports) and have their own ways of bundling and code splitting first with webpack and now vite. The popularity of Next.js Server Components blur the lines between where things run (server and client) in even more non-standard ways.

Would I want to see Next.js run perfectly with Deno? It would be interesting but what's the point? I think Next.js is kind of close to feeling like its own runtime, especially with the changes coming with [turbo pack](https://turbo.build/pack). Running Next.js outside of vercel already comes with it's own drawbacks, not benefiting from the way the infrastructure deploys the code.

The team has taken a pretty hard stance against ["build steps"](https://deno.com/blog/you-dont-need-a-build-step) for a myriad of reasons, it's slow, complicated, and obfuscates standard runtime operations. Once you start messing around with Deno Deploy, specifically the playground you see the the benefit of easily running a server in real-time with no build step, and it's really nice. It just feels simple. I think that Deno has the opportunity to think through the front-end landscape and implement its own suite of "standards" using web fundamentals, perhaps Web Components, module URLs, web APIs, etc. I think Fresh has great potential (even though I have problems with it too). I personally don't think of the use of preact as anything outside of a glorified JSX string renderer.

Just another thought, this should probably be in "discussions" and not an "issue". It might be best to close and migrate over to alleviate the "issues" backlog.

body: Not sure what you are looking for, but things have moved on.  The biggest is that Fresh is now a fully supported front end framework for Deno.  The Deno team have hired a full time maintainer Marvin Hagemeister, who is also a maintainer of Preact and generally super helpful chap.  As a framework, Fresh is probably easier to pick up than Next as it's less complex, unless you are coming from a strong Node/React background.  Deno will definitely offer a better developer experience than working with node/next.js, but is less popular.  There's a #fresh channel in the Deno Discord if you get stuck or have any questions.
author: cknight

body:
You probably made your decision already, but I have to add my thoughts here.

This post is still correct. Deno Fresh is 100% experimental and 100% **not** production ready. Go with NextJS or Remix. Use Deno for server if you want, but do not use Deno Fresh for front-end. It is **not** production ready.

- No graceful partial refreshes (hard refresh every time, for every tiny edit makes development slower and harder).
- Countless bugs with minimal reproducible code + videos that never get fixed.
- More, thoughtful issue reports that get deleted and ignored if minimal repo is not provided.
- Strange, wonky "not exactly bugs", minor issues in the routing system when your application gets more complex.
- Hard to understand Partials concept that people ask about all the time.
- Continuously delayed Fresh v2 that doesn't have any meaningful improvements over v1.
- Unfinished concepts that are still changing.

Use NextJS or Remix. We spent 5 months building our app in Fresh and we're now middle of migrating to NextJS. It's the biggest, most amateur mistake we ever made in our engineering.

author: markthree

body:
@CordialGit hey there - thanks for the questions!

1.) **Why Deno doesn't provide a framework like Oak directly in the runtime:** I think this is a fair question, and worth keeping in mind as the runtime evolves over time. One advantage of maintaining a lower-level HTTP server API is enabling innovation and experimentation in userland. If Deno were to provide an oak-like library for middleware, it would probably be in addition to the lower level API (increasing the API surface area), and probably have a chilling effect on any other third-party attempts to create a higher-level abstraction on top of HTTP requests. That being said, an API to create a middleware stack is a pretty fundamental component of backend servers, so maybe there's an argument to be made to add something like it to the standard library. We'll keep listening to the community for preferences here.

2a.) **Can I run Deno in production?** Short answer is yes. We have a number of customers (like Netlify, Supabase, and others) that are running large production workloads on Deno and Deno Deploy. Of course it's hard to give a blanket answer to whether or not Deno is appropriate for any particular use case, but you can draw some confidence from the fact that the runtime is being used in a non-trivial way by others.

2b.) **Support:** You are able to reach out to `support@deno.com` with critical or private issues, but given our relatively small team size, we do need to make difficult prioritization decisions with requests that come through this channel. If at all possible, I'd encourage you to use the `#help` channel in our [Discord community](https://discord.gg/deno), where you can receive help from both Deno team members and our user community. In the coming weeks, we will also restart Deno community office hours in Discord, where you can come to ask questions of Deno team members in real time.

3.) **Other hosts:** You can definitely use other hosts to run Deno applications! In these use cases, I would do as @markthree suggested, and [package your Deno server as a single executable](https://deno.com/manual/tools/compiler). If you generate an executable this way for your target platform (Linux, e.g.) you should be able to run it just about anywhere, [maybe using another server as a proxy](https://docs.nginx.com/nginx/admin-guide/web-server/reverse-proxy/).

author: lino-levan

body:
@lino-levan 
You see, JavaScript is powerful. It can do so much on the frontend. And it is a sufficient tool when now paired with Deno.

Now, I'm offended that creators of JavaScript frameworks are making JavaScript Frontend Development too difficult.

On the Frontend, I will take the pain to learn HTML, CSS and JavaScript. Then move forward to learn React and Next.JS.

If I choose the Vue route, I need to learn Vue, and Nuxt for Server Side Rendering, Pinia for Storage, Vuetify for Components, and Vitest for testing.

With so much frontend power in JavaScript already, we still learn more and more tools, in the name of wanting to simplify JavaScript on the Frontend.

**THE MATTER WITH DENO**
Considering the headache with Frontend, I am of the opinion that Backend should also not kill us.

All of these over-learning is making JavaScript Web Development too difficult.

And I'm hoping that Deno could just be the only thing we need to learn for Backend-- without the need for anything extra.

Yet all the full features that MiddleWare adds to the mix, will be self contained in Deno, out-of-the-box. This is the future I expect with Deno.

For the future, I am also hoping that some sensible JavaScript Developers would also come up with a **Single Complete Full Featured Frontend JavaScript Framework**-- which you can transition into, directly after learning Vanilla JavaScript, and it will have the capacity to handle State Management, Component creation, Testing, Styling, Server Side Rendering, Authentication, API, Validation, Deployment---and anything else that we need React and Next, or Vue, Nuxt and Pinia for.

I guess by now, you can see why my mind favours the one tool model?
author: CordialGit

body:
Just want to take a moment to say that I **don't** want that to be the future for Deno. I'm using Deno to build a CLI which does NOT need an http server and values keeping the compiled binary extremely small. Usage for CLI development has been the primary thing that attracted me to Deno, and I would hate to see its std library bloated when use cases like HTTP servers are easily accomplished with external libraries. 

Also, coupling too much into the std library would make it harder for the Deno ecosystem to evolve when improvements happen across the landscape for things like http servers. Forcing it into the std library ALSO forces Deno to become the maintainers of an http server and increases our dependence on them while increasing their burden. I'd rather they focus on making integration of modules/packages easier than have them "own" everything we all depend on.

body:
KV has basic support for maps by storing values as separate records under the same key prefix. You will need to choose a different prefix for each primary key or index. You can look up individual records by key or list all of them, in either forward or reverse order, in whatever sort orders your indexes allow.

Although it’s low-level compared to SQL, it doesn’t seem hard once you get used to it. You do need to update multiple records (the primary record and its index entries) using a transaction, which requires a loop for retries.

I don’t recommend putting a lot of data under a single key due to the 64k limit on values (on Deno Deploy) which seems quite low. Also, there are limits on how many records you can update in a transaction, so you wouldn’t want to create a lot of indexes for the same table.

The built-in operations are pretty primitive. I’ve seen a few attempts to make higher-level libraries on top of KV, but none seem popular yet. Maybe someone will build something with Redis-like operations?

body: We use both AWS and Google Cloud (for different things). They both have their strengths and weaknesses, depending on the use case. AWS typically exposes smaller pieces of a cloud system, which can be really helpful if you know exactly what you need and how you want it to work. Google Cloud tends towards a more "product-focused" cloud environment, meaning you get more complete solutions to the kinds of problems you have. In the case of Deno Deploy, we use both.

> I can just have a DNS entry on a custom domain that points to 127.0.0.1, or CNAMEs to another internal endpoint, or anything else. You can not protect against localhost access attacks using text based filtering of the hostname.

I researched this problem a lot, and it turns out to be not entirely true. There is such as thing as DNS rebinding protection that can prevent this. If you have DNS rebinding protection, then filtering the hostname is enough. This is at least a problem a sysadmin can solve, unlike accessing file URIs.
