I still strongly disagree, because to me it's like an early 2000s path traversal vuln, something you'd expect from PHP. And there is no good reason for it except that it's cute, so it's also just bloat. I was very surprised they allowed this. I continue to use [deno-safe-fetch](https://gitlab.com/soapbox-pub/deno-safe-fetch) in my projects.
author: alexgleason

body:
Update: the `--env-file` flag added to Deno in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/20300 fixes basically all my problems. With this I can use `--env-file --deny-read=.env` and it actually works.

The problem before was that I needed to allow reads to `.env` for the dotenv package to work. Doing so would inadvertently allow `fetch` to read it with a `file:` URI, forcing me to sanitize fetch inputs instead of relying on Deno permissions.

I still think it's a potential footgun. But I am more sympathetic to the idea it's a Deno permissions issue as long as the permissions API can actually address it. As long as people do it the new idiomatic way with `--env-file`, everything is fine.

body:
> Bun went 1.0 yesterday and seems to do most of what Deno does, and more. It apparently crushes Deno in performance, and they really made a smart move making it a drop in replacement for Node, and allowing mixing of ESM and CJS. Did Bun just make Deno obsolete? Why would I choose Deno instead of Bun right now? Any qualified opinions?

nodejs, bun and deno are all server-side js runtimes, but they have completely different goals.

As far as I know, the nodejs ecosystem is currently the best, and the other two runtimes are also compatible with the nodejs ecosystem, so they will converge in the future, but some nodejs libraries use magic that is unique to nodejs, which may be difficult to do in terms of compatibility.

bun is more concerned with performance, so it is much better than the other two runtimes in terms of performance right now.

deno is synonymous with safety in my opinion, I can safely use packages from the community without worrying about them doing things to my system that I don't know about.

node is now starting to make a big push on performance and security, too.

Competition is good, js runtime is starting to evolve

## Summary

If the project focuses on ecology → node
If the project focuses on performance → bun
If the project focuses on security → deno
author: markthree

body:
So you're saying if Bun implements all of Deno's security features, then Deno has no reason to live?

They don't really have "completely different goals" though, they are almost the same thing except a few differences in features. Some of Deno's seem to be more ethical than practical. To not be designed as a drop in replacement for Node requires a parallel ecosystem, which doesn't make it worth the switch from Node to Deno. But to switch from Node to Bun is almost a no-brainer if they deliver on what they promise.

author: raythurnvoid

body:
It's a mistake to assume that because there was a lot of effort and money put into something that will just automatically translate into better performance. It’s worth noting that React Native also chose JSC over V8 years ago because it was faster for their use case.

There are other things to consider, like where they focused the effort. Also, when we’re talking about speed, we’re actually talking about different types of speed since there are lots of tradeoffs to consider. 

- JSC prioritizes faster start times, while V8 prioritizes fast execution
- JSC has 3 optimizing compilers (more complex, but faster), while V8 has 2 optimizing compilers (less complex and easier to use, but not as fast)
- JSC’s architecture uses less memory but it executes code a little bit slower, V8 does more runtime optimization, but that takes more memory and slows startup time
- JSC outperforms V8 at regexp, setting properties, and some strings-related tasks whereas V8 tends to outperform at typed array access & promises.

But you are half right, it's not just about the performance of the underlying engine, but about the wrapping code, the FFI, and possibly a half-dozen other variables related to the architectural decision that each project made. Deno picked the heavier runtime engine, with a simpler, easier to use interface, and that decision most likely plays the single largest role in the outcome of the benchmarks that have been published.

It's not just some particular trick or optimization that they are doing to get more performance out of their underlying engine. It's just as simple as the engine choice to a large degree.
author: NeoVance

body:
> JSC outperforms V8 at regexp

Just a nitpick: RegExp is one of the places where V8 outclasses JSC (source: https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=258706)
author: aapoalas

body: A bug report about some specific case where regular expression compilation isn't JITed only proves that there are some specific cases where V8 can outperform JSC on regular expressions. It doesn't invalidate my claim, or give much weight to your own. There are some patterns that JSC doesn't have support for in the YARR JIT, but for the majority of commonly used patterns, JSC will be faster.

author: GroophyLifefor

body:
# ---

**The Evolution of JavaScript: From Browser Script to the Global Standard**

For over 15 years, the JavaScript ecosystem has undergone a transformation unlike any other in computing history. What began as a tool for simple browser animations has become the backbone of modern enterprise infrastructure.

## **1\. The Big Bang: Node.js and the V8 Revolution**

In 2009, the backend landscape was dominated by **Java, C++, PHP, and C\#**. These languages were powerful but often "heavy," requiring complex threading and high overhead.

**Ryan Dahl** changed everything by extracting the **V8 Engine** (the muscle behind Google Chrome) and applying it to the server.

* **The Paradigm Shift:** It introduced an event-driven, non-blocking I/O model.  
* **The Unified Stack:** For the first time, developers could use **one language** (JavaScript) for both client-side and server-side code.  
* **The Result:** Node.js became the "chassis"—the base car design upon which the modern web was built.

## ---

**2\. The Era of Maturity: TypeScript and Frameworks**

For years, "serious" developers viewed JS as a hobbyist scripting language. That changed when **Microsoft introduced TypeScript**. By adding static types, JS suddenly became as robust and predictable as Java or C\#.

This triggered a "boom" in professional tooling:

* **React (Meta/Facebook):** Revolutionized component-based UI.  
* **Angular (Google):** Brought enterprise-grade structure to the frontend.  
* **VS Code (Microsoft):** Built on JS (Electron), it became the world’s \#1 code editor.  
* **Vite/Vue:** Redefined how we bundle and build applications with lightning speed.

## ---

**3\. The Runtime Wars: Node vs. Bun vs. Deno**

As the ecosystem grew, the limitations of the original Node.js design (like the node\_modules "black hole") became apparent. This led to the rise of new competitors.

### **The Engine Room: V8 vs. JavaScriptCore (JSC)**

The core difference between these runtimes lies in the engine that executes the code:

| Feature | V8 (Node & Deno) | JavaScriptCore (Bun) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Origin** | Google (Chrome) | Apple (Safari) |
| **Strengths** | **Peak Throughput:** Optimized for long-running server tasks. | **Startup Speed:** Optimized for instant "cold starts" and low latency. |
| **Compilation** | Uses multiple tiers of JIT (Just-In-Time) compilation. | Uses a 4-tier JIT; generally more memory-efficient. |
| **Language** | Built in C++. | Built in C++, but Bun’s "wrapper" is built in **Zig**. |

### **The Car Analogy**

* **Node.js (The Reliable Base Car):** The foundation. It is the industry's defacto standard and has the largest library of parts (NPM).  
* **Bun (The Formula 1):** Built for raw performance. It includes a bundler, test runner, and package manager that are 10x-100x faster than Node's defaults. It aims for 90%+ compatibility with the "Base Car" design.  
* **Deno 2.0 (The Refined Sedan):** Also built by Ryan Dahl to fix Node's original flaws. While Deno 1.0 was "No-Node," **Deno 2.0** embraces the NPM ecosystem while offering a more stable, secure, and "batteries-included" experience.

## ---

**4\. The "Holy Grail" of Packages: NPM and JSR**

**NPM (Node Package Manager)** has become the "Holy Grail" library—the standard registry that every JS engine must support to survive. However, the industry is moving toward a "hardened" future:

* **JSR (JavaScript Registry):** The latest evolution. It is a modern, TypeScript-first registry that works across Node, Deno, and Bun. It aims to solve the security and speed issues of traditional NPM while remaining backward compatible.

## ---

**5\. Conclusion: The Defacto Standard**

JavaScript is no longer just for websites; it is the **defacto standard of web infrastructure**. We have reached a point where the "overhead" is gone, the engines are faster than ever, and the developer experience is unmatched.

Whether you are driving the **Reliable Chassis (Node)**, the **F1 Racer (Bun)**, or the **Refined Sedan (Deno)**, you are part of the most vibrant ecosystem in tech history.

author: EdamAme-x

body:
Kinda. 

JS/TS runs always single threaded. However, many things that happen under the hood, like network and IO activity are handled by different "system" threads in the Deno runtime. So your code does utilize some multithreading although the actual JS/TS itself runs single threaded.

People do often underestimate how light, fast, and powerfull this superficially single threaded async code can be. Well implemented async runtime does have great level of _concurrency_, even though there is no _parallel_ execution as folks typically understand it. For many typical web server tasks, this can be more efficient than true multithreading.

If your workload is more IO/database/network heavy, then you might be better off sticking to the "default" async execution, which is technically single threaded kinda, but can do a _lot_ of stuff concurrently. Oftentimes more than true multithreaded code, since there is all kinds of additional overhead involved in orchestrating parallel threads.

To actually, explicitly run JS/TS in a multithreaded way, you can use Web workers. Some modules or libraries you use may already do that under the hood.  Actual parallel execution using Web workers can be great for some workloads. Those are typically CPU-heavy or math-heavy blocking workloads like image processing. 

This is mostly the same in both Deno and Node.
author: jtoppine
git001

body:
> As I would like to work some upload tasks and every IO is blocking I would prefer to make it in dedicated worker.

Not sure what you mean by "every IO is blocking". IO in Deno is usually async, which means your JS/TS code is free to serve for example other requests while one request or any other async call is waiting for IO. When you await something in a serve handler, that particular response is effectively "blocked" until the promise is resolved, but meanwhile, Deno is free to continue executing _other_ async tasks, like serving other requests in the queue. It does not block all of your code. 

By quick look, all of your code here seems to be reasonably async, and using async streams. I suspect you wouldn't get much benefit from moving any of it to a worker thread. The code above should already be able to handle many, many concurrent requests with ease. 

I say "suspect" mainly because I'm not exactly sure how  _request.formData() internally works, more on that below. 

> Should I switch to the worker at that line `const formData = await _request.formData();` or inside the ` if (file) { ...}`, which I would prefer.

If there is any significant blocking happening, it would probably happen in the await _request.formData(). If that method internally reads all of the request body and parses it all at once to extract the uploaded files, then that would be a problem both for memory consumption and blocking the event loop for a significant period of time. If it's smart enough to create file streams dynamically in an async manner, then all good, you should be able to handle multiple requests concurrently even though it's technically single threaded. I'm guessing it's the latter since most everything in Deno tends to be nicely async in that manner. Maybe someone more knowledgeable can chime in on that.

body:
Run `deno info <url to the entry point>` and search for `https://deno.land/std@0.206.0/path/mod.ts`.

```text
Warning Implicitly using latest version (0.206.0) for https://deno.land/std/path/mod.ts
…
│ │ ├─┬ https://deno.land/x/gustwind@v0.54.3/utilities/fs.ts (900B)
│ │ │ ├─┬ https://deno.land/x/recursive_readdir@v2.0.0/mod.ts (513B)
│ │ │ │ └─┬ https://deno.land/std@0.206.0/path/mod.ts (2.26KB)
…
```

It reveals that the offending module is `recursive_readdir`. You can replace it with [`std/fs/walk.ts`](https://deno.land/std@0.206.0/fs/walk.ts).

body:
Deno can be successfully installed with this command, but there is still a warning.

```shell
cargo install --all-features --force deno --locked
```

the warning is:

```text
warning: package `ahash v0.8.3` in Cargo.lock is yanked in registry `crates-io`, consider running without --locked
```
author: kands-code

body: It's strange that it seems to have been solved today. Maybe it's because I used `--all-features` and didn't use `--locked`, so there will be different errors every time I try 😂
author: kands-code

body: `deno_runtime::worker::MainWorker` already [includes the `runtime` extension](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/v1.40.3/runtime/worker.rs#L432). The `extensions` field in `WorkerOptions` is used to provide additional extensions other than those built into `deno_runtime`.
author: 0f-0b

body: I recently started a new project to extend Deno STD. It encourages the addition of functionality that exists in other standard libraries (PHP, Go etc.), and also allows for functionality that benefits from being in a standard library rather than in third party modules. Here is the link: https://jsr.io/@stdext/crypto
author: halvardssm

body: I would love to see argon2 be included in the Deno std library to make password hashing as simple and fast as everything else in Deno is.
author: brettchalupa

body:
I have spent more time digging into password hashing and salting with Deno and came across https://jsr.io/@felix/argon2, a well-maintained library that uses Rust’s implementation of the algorithm. Unfortunately, it relies upon FFI which can’t be deployed to Deno Deploy as far as I can tell. 

I don’t see a simple way to implement secure password hashing, which is a requirement for most web application with auth, with Deno and Deno Deploy. 

If Deno provided a std lib implementation, that’d really make it more viable to use Deno and Deno Deploy for building full stack web apps with auth.
author: brettchalupa

body: For now I'm using https://jsr.io/@rabbit-company/argon2id which uses a WASM build of argon2 and is working on Deno Deploy. But it'd still be nice to have password hashing without FFI and without WASM.
author: brettchalupa

body: I suggest you take a look at https://jsr.io/@noble/hashes#argon2

author: huntercaron

body:
Sorry for digging up an old thread, but I felt the need to chime in.

See [dprint](https://github.com/dprint/dprint) and [dprint-plugin-typescript](https://github.com/dprint/dprint-plugin-typescript). It's the formatter Deno uses under the hood, created by absolute unit @dsherret. It's written in Rust, supports multiple runtimes with a WASM build, among other things. 

While `deno fmt` doesn't offer much in terms of configurability, `dprint` has configurability like no other. You can dial in its behavior with practically every type of change it applies. That granularity of control (and probably its performance, too) were partially responsible for it becoming the formatter of choice for TypeScript itself not too long ago. 

FYI: You can simplify the configuration process with the `deno: true` option in `dprint.json`. That pre-populates the rest of the configurations to the values that `deno fmt` uses. You can then override individual options as needed.

author: bartlomieju

body:
As a follow up to this. I haven't had success embedding Deno into my Rust application beyond integrating it to evaluate basic JavaScript. My efforts to fork Deno CLI or use the new/unstable deno_lib crate were plagued with issues so I gave up.

I was, however, able to compile Nodejs to a dynamic C library ([prebuilt binaries here](https://github.com/alshdavid/libnode-prebuilt)), [added Rust bindings](https://github.com/alshdavid/libnode_sys) and [a high level Rust AP](https://github.com/alshdavid/edon)I (with async Rust support).

I'm keeping up to date on Deno's progress and if things change I'll happily migrate to Deno. My hope is to compile Deno to a static or dynamic C library (to avoid collisions with dependencies), offer prebuilt binaries & rust bindings along with a high level Rust API.

Deno's opt system is quick fiddly, so I would probably rely on the Nodejs compat and write the high level API against Node's n-api

```typescript
import { S3, PutObjectCommandOutput } from "https://esm.sh/@aws-sdk/client-s3@3.513.0"
// import { S3, PutObjectCommandOutput } from "npm:@aws-sdk/client-s3@3.513.0"
import { AWSNodeCompatibleRequestHandler } from "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/gh/tazmaniax/common-code-parts@feat/update/AWSNodeCompatibleRequestHandler/mod.ts"

const denoRequestHandler = new AWSNodeCompatibleRequestHandler()

    accessKeyId: Deno.env.get('AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID') || '',
    secretAccessKey: Deno.env.get('AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY') ?? '',
    sessionToken: Deno.env.get('AWS_SESSION_TOKEN') ?? ''

    credentials: awsCredentials,
    requestHandler: denoRequestHandler
})

        }
    }
)
```

body:
This is my outsider view of this question, since it is always worth challenging assumptions.

There are a couple of reasons why this wouldn't make sense at this time:
1. swc is already a working integration in deno
2. oxc is under heavy development with breaking changes as late as 3 days ago (more than half a year after the question was posted)
3. VoidZero is a VC-funded project which could cause some surprising pivots

As is, it would probably make more sense to support the swc-project with perf.analysis and optimisations.

Competition is useful, but swapping one set of known issues for a new set of unknown issues may not provide the change one is hoping for.

body:
Some stuff that is missing in `oxlint` that makes switching right now difficult:
- No plugin support (they're working on it, but it's not yet released)
- No support for linting the module graph. Whilst not exposed as an API to users, our linter supports checking if a file is allowed to be imported from another file and stuff like that.

So yeah we would love to make the switch, but `oxlint` is not there just yet.

author: bartlomieju

body:
Ok I have made progress. Got my own custom Deno build integrated into my project with most of the Deno functionality included.

Things I've learned
 
1) You **_must_** have a snapshot to initialize the `JsRuntime` with
2) The extensions used by the `JsRuntime` must be the same (and same order) as the extensions used when creating the snapshot 
   1) I haven't tested this but I believe you can have extra extensions added at runtime, you just need to match the base extensions.
3) `MainWorker` provided by the `deno_runtime` crate provides the default extensions used by Deno, including the Node.js standard library
4) The `deno_*` crates do not provide an implementation for resolving `require` calls - [this can be extracted from the Deno cli](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/cli/npm/mod.rs#L49). It would be nice if Deno provided this as a standalone crate as it appears this is the only functionality I need to extract from the cli.
   1) I see `deno_npm`, perhaps that's what I am looking for

**Questions remaining:**
I copy/pasted the snapshot built from the Deno CLI crate into my crate - so I gotta figure out how to build it myself - it looks straightforward enough. I think I can copy most of the `build.rs` and change the references to point to `deno_runtime`.

Because of how long it takes to generate a release snapshot, how many extra dependencies it requires and how manageable the Deno release schedule is, I am considering if I should have the snapshots generated externally from my main repo - perhaps in a separate `alshdavid/deno_snapshots` repo - where I release platform specific precompiled Deno v8 snapshots as Github releases.

Consumers (just me at this stage) who want to integrate Deno into their project could download the snapshots into their project. I could also publish a crate that bottles up the Deno dependencies (npm manager, web_worker initialization) to simplify integration.

That should make it trivial to embed Deno into an existing Rust project
author: alshdavid

body:
Ok, so I have [created a repo](https://github.com/alshdavid/deno_embed/releases/tag/1.42.1) that publishes prebuilt Deno v8 snapshots from the cli (I hope this doesn't violate and licenses? it's all MIT from what I can tell). 

I feel this is valuable because building the snapshot makes it tricky to embed Deno into projects where we cross-compile in CI/CD. Using prebuilt snapshot binaries means that's not a concern plus it speeds up compile time.

If you call `createRequire()` from an JS/TS module, in wrappers for legacy CJS content, you usually place the value of `import.meta.url` as argument, to define the base of all relative path entries used in the later `require()` invocations.

First I was trying to enter the current working directory, but it needs a file location, not a directory, to work correctly.

Now I'm just using the `require(...)` target itself as argument for `createRequire` too. This seems to work and its simplicity looks quite attractive and helps to avoid lots of platform specific path handling issues. In this case you simply have to use filename of the script target prefixed by `./` and you have a sufficient relative link with minimal effort. But it's still not the correct way to solve this challenge.

Therefore, I'm looking around, if there could be a better solution without to much complexity.
author: mash-graz

body: The easiest and best solution would be to pass the value of `import.meta.url` to a Rust op - there's really no universal way to get this value in Rust.
author: bartlomieju

body:
Passing it from JS/TS looks like a realistic possibility, because you always have to prepare some mediating code on the TS/JS side anyway.

But this question isn't of vital significance anyway. I just puzzled about the problem and couldn't find an answer anywhere, but at the end that's more curiosity.

btw: I spend some time today to watch your video lectures about `deno` and its internal architecture and development. I learned a lot again! You really should continue communicating this kind of technical introductory lessons even after the Covid related video conference boom. It's extremely helpful for a better understanding of `deno`, its inner waking and code structure. Some topics (for example the `op2 macro, permission and state handling) are hard to grasp, if you don't get an explanation by those, who use them all the time.

You publish it as a regular package, specify a config like so:
```
{
  "name": "@scope/name",
  "version": "0.0.1",
  "exports": "./path/to/entrypoint.ts"
}
```

Then you can run it as: `deno run jsr:@scope/name`

author: alexgleason

body:
Not part of the team, just how I interpret earlier discussions about the topic:

Apparently the sqlite in Deno is considered an implementation detail, which could theoretically be changed or upgraded to something else, potentially not backwards compatible in the future. Exposing sqlite in the public API would mean that exact API would need to be kept and maintained indefinitely. 

Additionally; in general Deno attempts to stick to standardized web API's, and keep non-standard runtime features (like embedded sqlite would be) to a minimum.  Not sure but I think Deno supports some web standard db/storage features like localStorage/indexedDB and such (not super popular).

Additionally, and somewhat contrarily to the previous point, Deno already has another, custom embedded db, KV. Which works better with the proprietary Deno Deploy edge runtime service, where it is implemented using FoundationDB. On self-hosted deno, KV runs on top of the embedded sqlite.

I guess the thought process is to limit the amount of built-in storage solution API's to a minimum, and refer to KV or third party modules for other solutions.

All of that being said, although some of the rationale for not exposing it makes sense, I personally would like to see it happen nevertheless. I use sqlite with deno a lot and none of the available third party modules for it are perfect. sqlite via FFI requires wide open permissions, and the WASM version, while having served me well, is not exactly optimal. Being able to just utilize the embedded sqlite would be nice.

> But I am wondering what types are available other than "json"? The intellisense in vscode just says any for the whole with block and I can't seem to find the docs, which all seem to focus heavily on just importing json.

Currently only `"json"` type is supported. We did a bunch of preliminary work to support `text`, `bytes` and `wasm` types as well (eg.  https://github.com/denoland/deno_core/pull/402) but they are not yet available.

> I really have no idea if the above is possible, or if anyone is even thinking along these lines as an eventually feature?

There was talks about this in the team, especially @piscisaureus wanted such a feature, but so far there's been no consensus and we don't plan doing that in the forseeable future.

body:
The packages available on NPM, JSR and Deno Land are the ones made available by their authors. In today's age you should look for packages on JSR first. If not there then fall back to NPM. Deno Land only has packages that were made with Deno in mind or made compatible with Deno, so you might find some older packages on Deno Land but not JSR or NPM.

The purpose of JSR is to be a modern package registry for JavaScript. It won't have a package if the author of said package doesn't choose to publish on JSR. JSR is still relatively new, and Deno are more focusing their attention around JSR as it allows them to deduplicate dependencies between versions.

body:
> So if I want to run a program and pass it some stuff on stdin am I doomed to use the async interface, and thus make my wrapper for it async, and in turn to have to remember to await whateverWrapperFunc() every time I want to use it? I really hate that because sooner or later I'm going to forget to await and get a race.

If you want your JavaScript code to do literally anything while the Deno command is executing you can't use an event blocking function like outputSync. JavaScript is single threaded. You need to utilise promises so JavaScript can switch between tasks of getting the output of the Deno command, pushing input in and doing literally anything else while it waits.
author: BlackAsLight

body:
> > So if I want to run a program and pass it some stuff on stdin am I doomed to use the async interface, and thus make my wrapper for it async, and in turn to have to remember to await whateverWrapperFunc() every time I want to use it? I really hate that because sooner or later I'm going to forget to await and get a race.
> 
> If you want your JavaScript code to do literally anything while the Deno command is executing you can't use an event blocking function like outputSync. JavaScript is single threaded. You need to utilise promises so JavaScript can switch between tasks of getting the output of the Deno command, pushing input in and doing literally anything else while it waits.

I know, and I don't want it to.  In server context it often doesn't make any sense to want it to.  It's a shame to add promise/async/await to make parallelism simple and in the process make the even simpler approach (sequential operation) significantly *more* error-prone.  As far as I can tell node and now deno just inherited this lunatic setup from the browser which I guess is inevitable but doesn't stop me wishing it could be improved.  Most funcs aren't going to be intended for both sync and async use and in any case forcing the call-time default to one or the other based on the implementation detail of whether the func itself uses async stuff is unfortunate.  I realize there's a  small performance penalty in making a trip through the job queue but javascript glosses over similar inefficiencies all the time, and it would anyway be documented at the function definition end.
author: bkerin

body:
What exactly don't you like about the async await syntax? It allows you to write concurrent code in a sequential manner and makes it very easy to read and follow. JavaScript's single threaded-ness also makes handling race conditions insanely easy as you at exactly what points it may switch tasks; compared to some other languages where it may switch at literally any time during execution.

It also makes perfect sense in a server context to want promises; as the only thing that needs to be promises are things that rely on contexts outside the JavaScript environment.
author: BlackAsLight

body: Sure promises are great to have but forcing me to expose implementation use of them to all clients is annoying and error-prone.  Comments to this explain the issues with doing something better: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/47227550/using-await-inside-non-async-function they sort of boil down to it's too hard/inefficient to implement.  Given the situation iI think t's worthwhile to take the trouble to provide good synchronous API including with stdin being sent as someone mentioned above.
author: bkerin

body:
> Sure promises are great to have but forcing me to expose implementation use of them to all clients is annoying and error-prone.

What do you mean by this? `expose implementation`? If the error you're worried about running into is having to convert a function to async and then all of the functions that call it to async, then the solution to that is really just experience. When writing functions in the future you should be able to tell if it's going to need to call some async aspect and won't ever even need to be converting past sync functions to async functions. 

> Comments to this explain the issues with doing something better: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/47227550/using-await-inside-non-async-function they sort of boil down to it's too hard/inefficient to implement.

Reading the accepted answer should explain why you can't await a promise in a non-async function. 

Functions like Deno.readFileSync, prompt, alert, don't return promises. They do something different that pauses the execution of JavaScript. 

> Given the situation iI think t's worthwhile to take the trouble to provide good synchronous API including with stdin being sent as someone mentioned above.

You shouldn't use sync APIs within a server context. Users will experience random lag spikes for no apparent reason.
author: BlackAsLight

body:
> > Sure promises are great to have but forcing me to expose implementation use of them to all clients is annoying and error-prone.
> 
> What do you mean by this? `expose implementation`? If the error you're worried about running into is having to convert a function to async and then all of the functions that call it to async, then the solution to that is really just experience. When writing functions in the future you should be able to tell if it's going to need to call some async aspect and won't ever even need to be converting past sync functions to async functions.

No, it's just a javascript limitation.  It doesn't provide a way to hide the implementation detail that a function uses async code.  If you're going to have syntactic sugar for promises there's no great reason not to let people hide this, it was just too hard.

> 
> > Comments to this explain the issues with doing something better: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/47227550/using-await-inside-non-async-function they sort of boil down to it's too hard/inefficient to implement.
> 
> Reading the accepted answer should explain why you can't await a promise in a non-async function.

I did read it.  I get it.  The comments by the answers author further address the issue which comes down to 2x code per layer. 

> Functions like Deno.readFileSync, prompt, alert, don't return promises. They do something different that pauses the execution of JavaScript.
> 
> > Given the situation iI think t's worthwhile to take the trouble to provide good synchronous API including with stdin being sent as someone mentioned above.
> 
> You shouldn't use sync APIs within a server context. Users will experience random lag spikes for no apparent reason.

It's entirely a matter of the particular application whether this matters even slightly

body:
Try executing just this line: `import createFastify from 'npm:fastify@^4';`
```
deno run main.js
✅ Granted env access to "NODE_V8_COVERAGE".
✅ Granted env access to "V8_COVERAGE".
✅ Granted sys access to "hostname".
```

These three are getting asked by `fastify` (or its dependencies).

The `<main_module>` permissions is queried if you use `Deno.mainModule` or `process.argv`.

> Finally, Deno requests read access to "/home/kaki/.cache/deno/node_modules" is plain weird, like as if import statements required permissions, which doesn't sound right, but if I reject anyway then the script still works, and if I try --allow-read="/home/kaki/.cache/deno/node_modules" then it still prompts for it...

No, these permissions are only asked by `require()` calls - because they accept any JS expression they can't be statically analyzed and we need to query for permissions there. Most likely the `require()` call is wrapped in `try {} catch {}` and that's why the program still works. Some libraries do that to try and load a certain library (eg. `v8-code-cache` or `source-maps)`.

Hope that helps.

body:
This is an interesting issue, I also encountered it with some other package I import from the npm registry.
What I don't get is why I never had encountered this before while I was using npm modules many times, I would assume that deno would use the registry cache all of the time.

So from what I read here I conclude: The reason for the permission requests are the require-calls inside the npm package. This might be because usually dependencies are gathered by deno before actually running deno with the specific restricted permissions. But on require it is done at runtime when the permissions are already restricted. 

- Is this correct?
- What is the the good way to fix it?
    - always adding -A in this case? (Though I want most exact permissions)
    - specifying different permissions depending on which packages I use? (Though I don't want to upgrade my tasks with these things whenever I change my dependencies, as changing dependencies happens fast in development - this would be a chronic pain )
    - Update all my dependenceis to not use require? (Phew, this might be a lot of work but maybe worth it in some cases)
    
    
I think from it's nature it is not possible to change this behavior from within deno. Or well "possible" it might be a bad design choice as generally require behaves like an run-time read. You may write out arbitrary JS files and then parse them via require - okay wait, how are dynamic imports much different?...
author: JhonnyJason

body:
> You may write out arbitrary JS files and then parse them via require - okay wait, how are dynamic imports much different?...

`import ...` and `import()` are statically analyzable (with limits like `import("" + `${var}`) compared to `require()` which can be aliased to a single letter variable or something else that's obscure.

author: marvinhagemeister

body:
I think you're taking the `task` concept a bit too much to the extreme. If you want to fire a one-off command for `npm:create-svelte@latest`, then you should just do that by calling `deno run -A npm:create-svelte@latest`. Whilst you can also put this in a task, it would be kinda pointless.

The motivation behind tasks is to abstract common complex tasks that you need to run daily. A typical use case is that there is a `dev` task that spins up your app in development mode, sets up file watching and maybe does other work needed to launch your application. Let's use a made up example and pretend that this would be the command to launch your app in development mode:

```sh
deno run -A --watch-hmr my-app.ts
```

Now you could of course always type out the whole command by hand yourself. This gets very tedious though. You could put this in a shell file, but then have to deal with different developers using different shells and operating systems, which all have a different syntax. Windows has PowerShell, macOS has zsh and linux typically has bash or sometimes fish. It's not something you want to bother with as a developer, and neither should your team mates.

So what if you could just call `deno taks dev` instead? That way you don't need to remember the exact command for a project. Most projects have different commands for this and having to remember every one of them is annoying, especially if you're working in a team that works on many project at the same time.

We can solve this headache by adding a `dev` task to all projects. That way developers on the different projects don't need to remember whether this projects needs to copy some files first before launching, how to set up the file watcher for this particular project, what the entrypoint name for a certain project is, etc. The list of things to remember gets pretty long the more complex a project grows.

This all can be solved with tasks. Let's add a task for development setup of our imaginary project:

```json
{
  "tasks": {
    "dev": "deno run -A --watch-hmr my-app.ts"
  }
}
```

This means now you can just call `deno task dev` and don't have to remember anything else. In other words: The task feature is only there fore convenience.  It allows you to abstract away more complex commands in something that's easier to remember. In most projects you'll find a `build`, a `start` or a `dev` task respectively. As you discovered, they make no sense for one off things like bootstrapping a new project. That said, nothing prevents you from creating a task out of any `deno` command. You can literally put anything in a task. Whether that is a useful thing to do is a different question.
author: marvinhagemeister

body:
@marvinhagemeister Thank you for your answer, but I think I did not write my question well because of my confusion, so maybe the following will shed some light:

- I appreciate your refresher on tasks because I was indeed worried I was misunderstanding tasks, and now I think what you wrote confirms for me that conceptually `deno task` is not what I should be using in order to run `npm:create-svelte@latest` (because a task is something *we define* as a "macro" within the context of a project)
  - if you are not in the context of a Deno project, then it does not make sense to define a task in order to run `npm:create-svelte@latest`; you just want to run `deno run -A npm:create-svelte@latest` once (it is a simple, one-off command)! 
- I fell into this confusion because, about two weeks ago I ran into the issue where: `deno run -A npm:create-svelte@latest` was throwing an error:
```
error: Could not find a matching package for 'npm:create-svelte@latest' in a package.json file. You must specify this as a package.json dependency when the node_modules folder is not managed by Deno.
```
- Apparently, according to [someone who responded on Discord](https://discord.com/channels/684898665143206084/684898665151594506/1280295285385461903), this was due to https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/25232 and they created for me the following issue: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/25373
  - I am not entirely sure yet, but I think they might have misunderstood, and I should not have let them create the issue and instead created one myself? 
- In any case, the discussion in that confused me. It seemed to me that instead of `deno run -A npm:create-svelte@latest` (as I would usually do) I was supposed to use instead use `deno task`??? I found it difficult to understand lucacasonato's [comment](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/25373#issuecomment-2326110544):

> We've discussed this internally, and this is working as intended.

> deno run always prioritizes running code, which means it first and foremost treats it's arguments as file paths. For example you also can not run a task named ./task.ts with deno run, as it will attempt to load that file. Only if a task does not look like a file path or module specifier will we attempt to look for tasks and run them.

> Instead you should use deno task to run tasks like this (or better, rename your task!).

- the point that confused me was this last sentence "Instead you should use deno task to run tasks like this, or better yet, rename your task"...
  - and I think this is because the issue author thought my problem was coming from trying to run `deno task` with `npm:create-svelte@latest`? 
  - I am not sure, but there is some miscommunication somewhere


In any case, what would help me right now is to get some clarity on:
  - why is it that I was getting that error when running `deno run -A npm:create-svelte@latest`? 
  - why is it that I am no longer getting that error? Can I just go on with using `deno run -A npm:create-svelte@latest` as if none of the intervening stuff happened?
author: bzm3r

body:
> why is it that I was getting that error when running `deno run -A npm:create-svelte@latest`?

That was a bug that we fixed. There shouldn't be any error.

> why is it that I am no longer getting that error?

Because we fixed that bug. It occurred because we also added support for running `tasks` defined in `deno.json` via `deno run` to make it easier for folks swichting from `npm`. We missed an important edge case leading to the weird behavior you described.

> Can I just go on with using `deno run -A npm:create-svelte@latest` as if none of the intervening stuff happened?

Yes, please do. That's the way to do it 👍

body:
> And the 2nd question is: does the API deno_core::JsRuntime::run_event_loop only run 1 tick? or it try to wait everything inside its internal queue to complete?

No, it runs for as long as event loop finishes all work. If you want to run a single "tick" you should use [`JsRuntime::poll_event_loop`](https://docs.rs/deno_core/latest/deno_core/struct.JsRuntime.html#method.poll_event_loop).

I am a little confused how to wait for it done?

Or, it only returns `Ready` when done (just like `run_event_loop` is done), and for other cases it always returns `Pending`? correct?
author: linrongbin16

body:
> Or, it only returns Ready when done (just like run_event_loop is done), and for other cases it always returns Pending? correct?

Yes, that's correct - if it returns `Poll::Pending` you should call `JsRuntime::poll_event_loop` some time in the future - in your case inside the `tokio::select!` macro.

author: marvinhagemeister

body:
> load_main_es_module uses the [v8::script_compiler::compile_module](https://docs.rs/v8/latest/v8/script_compiler/fn.compile_module.html) to compile the main module, and also created all the module dependencies from it (like a dependency tree). At this time, all the modules that stores on local file system is synchronizely loaded, all the remote modules that need to be downloaded is still in fetching status.

By the time `load_main_es_module` all remote modules need to be downloaded and loaded into the V8 as well

> run_event_loop start to running the event loop, so all the remote modules can be downloaded, all the async and Promise can be resolved, until the javascript code in the main module been all executed to done.

Remote modules are already downloaded, this function keeps going as long as there are async actions pending (think timers, reads from net socket, etc).

> Also here's a guess: the javascript code in a v8 Script is pure tranditional js, it cannot use require or import keywords to import other modules. Correct?

Correct

body:
This is supported now. You can run a a task on a specific project using `deno task -f <project> <command>`

Here is the full output from `deno help task` that specifies all the relevant options

```
Options:
      --cwd <DIR>        Specify the directory to run the task in
  -f, --filter <filter>  Filter members of the workspace by name
  -r, --recursive        Run the task in all projects in the workspace
```

author: serujin

body:
> I mean, in a js runtime such like node/deno, the event loop will block the main thread and wait for callbacks trigger it, not forever loop and use 100% CPU core.

That's correct - only if there's some scheduled work the event loop is blocking. If there is no scheduled work then nothing can push more microtasks onto the queue.

> deno_core implement the queueMicrotask API by itself. i.e. it creates a queue inside rust side, and expose an ops API (for example ops.queue_microtask) to javascript side, then set globalThis.queueMicrotask = function(callback)  { ops.queue_microtask(callback);  }. So deno_core can get notified when the queue is triggered.

Not really, when microtask is queued it uses the `Isolate::enqueue_microtask` API and V8 handles them internally in its queue.

> The micro tasks never need to trigger the event loop to run (which should be a standard behavior specified in ECMAScript), it always simply wait to run in the next tick triggered by other Promise.

Correct. There must be some other scheduled work that can queue microtasks and potentially schedule more work for the future.

body:
When you import a `node:*` module in Deno, then you import code from Deno that mirrors the Node APIs. It doesn't shell out to Node itself. Therefore Node is not required when using Deno.

If you want to write a library that works in both runtimes, only going with `node:*` imports is one way to do that.

body:
I believe there's more to consider than just raw performance. The LevelDB ecosystem isn't as robust, user-friendly, and established as SQLite. This makes SQLite a safer choice to avoid a lot of headaches.

Finally, if you are really settled on the idea of using LevelDB (and you need to handle 50k writes/sec in a single box), you can implement a Deno KV backend using LevelDB.

BTW: Deno KV uses a FoundationDB backend in Deno deployment.
author: Zizaco

body:
I understand there are many factors involved, such as ACID,  partitioning, concurrency etc. Since DenoKV in Deno deployment utilizes FoundationDB, which is also an embeddable key-value database(if I am not wrong), my question revolves around why they chose SQLite over an embeddable KV database for their local APIs.

While SQLite is well-established so does the  LevelDB and I agree the key differences is user-friendliness and community support. From a high-level perspective, SQLite is just an implementation detail here; users and developers interact with it through Deno bindings, so it doesn't make much difference what database is used under the hood.

I believe it would be beneficial for the developers to provide documentation or guidance on extending DenoKV to support other key-value databases. Currently, DenoKV with SQLite doesn't perform well as needed in production, although it works great in local environments.
author: thelovekesh

body: We chose SQLite because it was already a dependency in Deno used in other places (eg. Web Cache API) and didn't want to bring yet another dependency. After considerations it came out that SQLite is sufficient for local version of Deno KV.

author: redradist

body:
Problems with `http` imports:
- Diffused trust model: No guarantee that third party domains stay up. We received several security reports over the years where a domain changed ownership
- No way to de-duplicate dependencies. If two dependencies each pull in a shared dependency, like `dep-A` importing `shared@1.0.0` and `dep-B` importing `shared@1.0.6` you'll always end up with two copies of `shared`. This breaks lots of popular libraries like `react`. Also means that it's difficult to update a sub-dependency if it's imported by many other dependencies and keep them in sync.
- No way to use an import map in a library.
- No package boundary, meaning LSP will display every type from every file, even if a library intended it to be internal. Autocompletion is often useless for that reason if you're working on a bigger project with many http dependencies.

Advantages of JSR over `http` imports:
- Full proof of publishing through provenance attestation
- Libraries can use their own import maps and have it work out of the box
- Proper semver de-duplication, no more duplicated dependencies if version ranges match
- No more noise in autocompletion, you'll only see the types intended to be used
- automatic docs generation (nice touch mostly)
- Code on JSR can be pulled into other runtimes too, not just Deno.

We've written a bunch of blog posts on this topic which might interest you:
- https://deno.com/blog/http-imports
- https://deno.com/blog/jsr_open_beta
- https://deno.com/blog/std-on-jsr
author: marvinhagemeister

body:
@marvinhagemeister Okay, I would like provide my opinion on you description and @ry 

>     * Full proof of publishing through provenance attestation
Agree on it

>     * Libraries can use their own import maps and have it work out of the box
It could also be possible to implement with `http-imports` if `deno` search for library "https://deno.land/std@0.224.0/assert/mod.ts" at path "https://deno.land/std@0.224.0/assert/import-maps.json" (in 'known-well locations')

>     * Proper semver de-duplication, no more duplicated dependencies if version ranges match
With range of versions agree, it pretty hard to do right

>     * No more noise in autocompletion, you'll only see the types intended to be used
As for me it is subjective, maybe I like having more information

>     * automatic docs generation (nice touch mostly)
Nice thing, but `deno` could provide additional command to generate documentation for library, and it could be hosted on github for example

>     * Code on JSR can be pulled into other runtimes too, not just Deno.
Agree on it


Ryan Dahl say the very intersting thing in article https://deno.com/blog/http-imports:

"
Under the hood, JSR still uses HTTP imports. For example, take this specifier:

jsr:@luca/flag

The above can really just be thought of as a smart redirect to:

https://jsr.io/@luca/flag/1.0.0/mod.ts
"

@marvinhagemeister What if I want still to use urls even with `jsr`, could you on site of `jsr` also provide this redirect url ?
For example I do not want to use "jsr:" specifier because it is not browser compatible and also do not want to use extra step `deno bundle` for bunding it to single `.js` file with all "jsr:" specifiers resolved ...

Could you provide redirection url for such use-case ?
author: redradist
redradist

body:
Well known locations are brittle for module URLs. You never know what part is the registry and what is the package. Like with `https://example.com/foo@1/foo@2` Is the package `foo@1` or `foo@2`?

Our experience over the past years with `http` imports has shown that third party domains get taken over by different entities leading to security issues. We cannot have that in the Deno ecosystem.

No, you cannot use `http` URLs for JSR directly. Doing so would break the module boundary and package import maps. What Ryan means there is that we download files instead of one big package tarball like with npm.

What you're describing with "locked" import maps is basically what we do with `deno.lock`. There is no spec for nested import maps in the browser though. There is a proposal that's being worked on, but it's far too early to adopt and unsure if it will ever succeed.
author: marvinhagemeister
redradist

body:
> What if I want still to use urls even with jsr, could you on site of jsr also provide this redirect url ?
> For example I do not want to use "jsr:" specifier because it is not browser compatible and also do not want to use extra step deno bundle for bunding it to single .js file with all "jsr:" specifiers resolved ...

Even if you could and did use http imports from JSR, it wouldn't work in the browser as JSR would be serving TypeScript files, which isn't compatible with the browser. 

> Our experience over the past years with http imports has shown that third party domains get taken over by different entities leading to security issues. We cannot have that in the Deno ecosystem.

An example of this is with what happened with the polyfil lib. The domain expired, somebody else bought it and changed it to serve malware instead to millions of users browsers.

@BlackAsLight 
> > What if I want still to use urls even with jsr, could you on site of jsr also provide this redirect url ?
> > For example I do not want to use "jsr:" specifier because it is not browser compatible and also do not want to use extra step deno bundle for bunding it to single .js file with all "jsr:" specifiers resolved ...

Yes, it will not work on browser without compilation, but original idea to use import for production would be live.
I very like idea of using imports, but as was mentioned previously on this disscution, raw import could not support multiple versions like other package manager.

That is why I think it could be possible to bring ideas from both side, use "jsr:" during development, but on production allow to use raw imports by generating `deno-import-maps-lock.json`
author: redradist

body:
@marvinhagemeister 
Oh my !!
Looks like you removed `deno bundle` in `Deno 2.0` ... (

My opinion, it should not be done even thought `deno bundle` (default one) was not ready yet, what instead should be done is SOLID principle.

I would use 'O' - Open-Close principle, leave `deno bundle` (with default behavior), but also add possibility to configure external bundlers, for example:
```bash
deno bundle main.ts # Default deno bundler

deno bundle --set-bundler esbuild # Deno start using esbuild bundler and start searching esbuild customization scripts
deno bundle main.ts # esbuild bundler

deno bundle --set-bundler webpack # Deno start using webpack bundler and start searching webpack customization scripts
deno bundle main.ts # webpack bundler
```
author: redradist

body: The bundler in Deno had been unmaintained for years and it's too expensive to maintain for us. We removed it for now, but we are considering bringing it back at some point. The plan is to base it on `rspack` or `rolldown` if that happens instead of writing our own bundler.

author: kuchta

body: So `npm:<package_name>` is a "short-hand" form, that is not really a URL, but `npm:/<package_name>` is a valid URL. We do that conversion internally to not bother users with it, and `deno info` outputs it with slash so you can actually parse them as URLs.
author: bartlomieju

body: Thank you for clarification, Bartek. But actually both are valid URLs (URIs) parsable by `new URL()` just one with pathname starting with a slash while the other is not which is kind of confusing considering they are not absolute paths, but I think if you generally prefer one form over the other it's better to be consistent in the output with the recommended input (interface)
author: kuchta

body:
> Thank you for clarification, Bartek. But actually both are valid URLs (URIs) parsable by `new URL()` just one with pathname starting with a slash while the other is not which is kind of confusing considering they are not absolute paths, but I think if you generally prefer one form over the other it's better to be consistent in the output with the recommended input (interface)

Neither start with a slash. It starts with `npm:/`. What I believe he is saying, like the HTTPS links starts with `http://`, this link starts with `npm:/`.
author: BlackAsLight

body: No, URLs (URIs) don't start with `scheme:/` and neither with `scheme://` unless there's a optional authority (host) section present. See: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/URL ``` > new URL('npm:/tailwindcss@3.4.14') URL { href: "npm:/tailwindcss@3.4.14", protocol: "npm:", pathname: "/tailwindcss@3.4.14", } > new URL('npm:tailwindcss@3.4.14') URL { href: "npm:tailwindcss@3.4.14", protocol: "npm:", pathname: "tailwindcss@3.4.14", } ```
author: kuchta

body:
If the path of a URL does not start with a slash, it is said that the URL has an opaque path. The ways you can work with such URLs are very limited. For example when resolving a relative URL, if the base URL has an opaque path, the input can only be a `#fragment` or an absolute URL.

```js
new URL("bar", "npm:package/foo").href; // throws. "bar" is neither a fragment or an absolute URL
new URL("bar", "npm:/package/foo").href; // returns "npm:/package/bar"
```

I guess this is why [the change](https://github.com/denoland/deno_semver/pull/6/files#diff-89ebfcfeb012ed523fb1f8ba30649100bbd8444269d867ce726cc646e6789823L500-R494) was made.

body: What do you mean the same ? My questions is about why `process` global exists, why not provide automatic conversion with `deno lint fix` or add additional flag `--sloppy-node-globals` or `--sloppy-node-process`
author: redradist

body:
@idleberg Looks like deno team listened feedback and added flag `--unstable-node-globals` https://deno.com/blog/v2.1#improved-support-for-nodejs-globals
I think they should now move `process` global under this new flag when it become stable --node-globals`
author: redradist

body:
> Personally I think support of `process` global by default is mistake, I think it should be only supported in imported modules using `npm:` or in `*.cjs` files like it done for `require` method ...

This has been tried for several years and resulted in endless stream of problems for users. I wrote about it more in https://deno.com/blog/v2.0-release-candidate#changes-to-global-variables. There's a ton of frameworks that load configuration files that the first thing they do is access `process.env`. We made good strides with several frameworks to do explicit `import process from "node:process";` before accessing `process`, but there's a lot of existing code out there that doesn't do that.

> Such support of global `process`, broke detection of runtime. 

How did it break the detection?


> Also it make no sense why support one feature and not support other, and where is the boundary between `deno` and `node` runtime ...

At this point, with recent addition of https://github.com/denoland/deno/commit/1a0cb5b5312941521ab021cfe9eaed498f35900b the only difference is that Deno defaults to ESM instead of CJS if there's no `type` option specified. Yeah, it's different than what Node is doing, but we want to push ecosystem forward to using ESM and our support of CJS is a great opportunity here.

redradist

body:
`node:cluster` is supported as of **Deno 2.8.0**. `cluster.fork()` and the primary/worker lifecycle were implemented in #33752 (shipped in 2.8.0), so the `Not implemented: cluster.fork` error you hit on 2.0.2 no longer occurs. Libraries built on `node:cluster` (e.g. `throng`, cc @rmcarias) work now.

Please `deno upgrade` to 2.8.0 or newer and give it another try. One caveat: the implementation currently targets Unix; Windows support is still limited. If you run into a specific gap, please open a new issue with a repro.

body:
Hey, thanks for raising this.

> That's fine to me, but it makes it hard for me to decide on whether Deno is a good idea for us.

You're not the only one and we are planning to address this more concretely this quarter.

> What if you started openly working on getting Node's test suite to pass using Deno?

This has been well underway - we are actually running some tests from Node.js test suite - you can see it here: https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/tests/node_compat/config.jsonc. Admittedly, it's barely ~20% of all tests available in Node and we have some plans to improve it - mainly by running these tests out-of-band, in a separate repository so we can always run all tests and report which ones are actually broken and which ones are now passing. And obviously a frontend page will be added as well to keep track of the status based on Deno release.

> Right now you do indeed display which features of which module isn't supported, but what's supported isn't necessarily one-to-one with what actually works. I used many Bun APIs for instance that they said were supported, but due to bugs, it ended up not actually working with particular libraries etc.

We also have another project called `npm_smoke_tests` that runs various popular npm libraries under Deno and tests if they are working properly. This one is not currently public as it needs some more polish, but then again the plan is to make it open source and widely available.

body:
Your list doesn't exclude `fixtures`, for example. The check [here](https://github.com/ffMathy/is-deno-compatible-yet/blob/65417168c165c56e37660b3035c43fb7c697f670/update.ts#L48) needs to be something like:

```js
!category || ignoredDirectories.some(dir => category.startsWith(dir))
```

because categories includes items like `fixtures/wpt/url`, `fixtures/es-modules/json-cache`, etc. On the other hand, `ignoredDirectories` list only includes `fixtures`.

Our counting is visible here: https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/1d49b3cb0f54eb8184acc00ec4bb3bd519653441/tests/node_compat/runner/TODO.md

In our current calculation, there are 3681 files in total and 595 files are ported for now.

body:
I had a similar need for embedding executables as I am experimenting bundling a small webview executable with my Deno application.  The workaround I came up with is to first write the executable to the filesystem before running `Deno.Command`.

```ts
const file = Deno.readFileSync(import.meta.dirname + "/webview/build/bin/webview-linux-x86_64")
Deno.writeFileSync(`/tmp/webview`, file, { mode: 0o755 })
new Deno.Command(`/tmp/webview`).spawn()
```

Alternative example using file stream
```ts
const file = await Deno.open(import.meta.dirname + "/webview/build/bin/webview-linux-x86_64")
await Deno.writeFile("/tmp/webview", file.readable, { mode: 0o755 })
new Deno.Command("/tmp/webview").spawn()
```

Reminder to `--include` the resource when compiling:
```sh
deno compile --allow-read --allow-write --allow-run --include "webview/build/bin/webview-linux-x86_64" main.ts
```

You could look into `Deno.makeTempFile()` to make it more OS-agnostic.

body: FYI: Both Deno and Node are built around V8 JS engine which is why the debugger integration is the same. It's provided by V8, not by Deno or Node. So there is no copying anywhere because both projects directly use the one provided by V8.
author: marvinhagemeister

body: V8 gives you the [inspector protocol](https://v8.dev/docs/inspector), which you can build a client on. Deno "built" the exact same client as https://github.com/node-inspector/node-inspector did, and in the exact same wrong way. And the funniest part being that [Bun](https://github.com/oven-sh/bun) also [did the exact same thing](https://bun.sh/docs/runtime/debugger#inspect)  as Deno did...
author: thoughtsunificator

body:
I'm sorry, am I missing something here? Are debuggers a taboo? I've never heard of a religion against debugging though... 

I guess that's it for deno.
author: thoughtsunificator

body:
> Start the debugger (CLI) as soon as the debugger keyword is met, it can't be that impossible that even 3 runtimes later we can't have a reasonable debugging experience.

The way V8 debugger works is you need to have client connected to stop on a `debugger` keyword, and it's already possible when you're running code from your IDE like VSCode or JetBrains. You can start a debugging session and when the `debugger` keyword is hit they will pause execution and let you inspect the state of program.
author: bartlomieju

body:
I believe I understand some parts of the architecture of it which lead me to make it clear that these are concerns that should be addressed on the runtime side and certainly not IDE side or engine side for that particular matter.

I've read some parts of the source code of the current Node.js and I pretty much get why this sucks, although it'd be a little bit harder for me to offer a solution to this (vital) problem plus the fact that I don't want to ask anyone to refactor its entire codebase for such a core feature that would only, be... mean.

So basically, any developer that expects any form of debugging experience through the most primitive tools such as CLI is asked to develop (and maintain) its own debugger client and work on the interoperability of at least a dozen of tools across the toolchains, am I right?
author: thoughtsunificator

body:
> So basically, any developer that expects any form of debugging experience through the most primitive tools such as CLI is asked to develop (and maintain) its own debugger client and work on the interoperability of at least a dozen of tools across the toolchains, am I right?

I believe Node has a CLI based debugger with `node inspect script.js`, Deno doesn't have it, because no one has ever asked for a CLI debugger. I'm not sure which dozen tools across toolchains you mean. I believe all JS runtimes understand and speak V8 debugging protocol, so that would be the only thing needed to support then.
author: bartlomieju

body:
In the less bad scenario, you still need to explicitly ask the run time to activate its debugging client. which is exactly what I advocated against in the first place. 

This does not work well because you actually need to ask twice for the debugger client, one using the CLI and two by putting a debugger statement in your code, the other reason why this might not work so well is these other tools I talked about, they might not care about starting a debugger which mean anything that you build on top off breaks this precise behavior. 

At this point, Deno just brought the same failures from node-inspector.
author: thoughtsunificator

body: It seems like you have a well defined vision in your head of how you'd like the debugger to work. Have you considered making a PR?
author: marvinhagemeister

body: Well... Maybe I would if anyone could try and explain to me why it was done that way :)
author: thoughtsunificator

body:
> Well, maybe I would if anyone could explain to me why it was done that way.

Because connecting a debugger client requires spawning a WebSocket server, which is not free to create and binds to a certain port (`127.0.0.1:9229` by default). Most users don't want a WS server being spawned in their production environment and that's why it's not done unless you provide a flag to inform you'd like to connect a debugger.
author: bartlomieju

body:
Then how about an env instead? Which would, at the very least, fix my second issue (and the issue you mentioned). 
How about `NODE_ENV` for example, which already exists by the way. 

The bind thing is a non-issue, as Deno is already aware that both the client and the server originates from a single command: `deno run foo.js` in a `NODE_ENV` that is for sure not set to production.

I mean, that's the whole point of it, if we're trying to accommodate our DX to production needs we're not going to go far.

**I propose that either a new env is added or we use `NODE_ENV` which seems like a pretty good use of it. When set, ANY script shall be debuggable either using the internal debugger client (CLI) or simply by providing the server, depending on the execution context.**

Deno looks for `deno.json` and `package.json` files in you CWD and up the parent directory tree. If it finds either (or both) of these files it will use `imports` and `dependencies` mappings to figure out what's the import, you can read more about it here: https://docs.deno.com/runtime/fundamentals/modules/

> Also what happens when the package is using some unsupported API, will Deno give me an error at startup, or will it be later at runtime?

It will error when a function that is unsupported is used, so at runtime.

body:
You can skip adding a version if:
- you're writing a simple app or one of script
- you are prototyping something
- don't care if your app keeps working in the future (because it's a prototype etc)

You should _always_ use a version or version constraint together with a lockfile if:

- more than one dev work on the code
- the app should keep working in the future
- you don't want to spent time debugging why the app works on machine A, but not on machine B (this typically happens because of different dependency version installed on the users machine. A lockfile prevents this)

We strongly recommend going with the versioned approach for any serious project. Only consider going without versions if it's a short lived prototype that only a single developer hacks on.

body:
This is solved by both libraries specifying a version range instead of an exact version. Example:

- `lib-b` depends on `lib-c@^1.0.0` (note the `^` character)
- your app depends on `lib-c@^1.1.0` (again, note the `^` character)

This will install `lib-c` only once and both your app and `lib-b` will share the exact same version. This will always be the case for as long as the version ranges match. A scenario where they would not match would be where your app depends on `lib-c@^3.0.0` but `lib-b` depends on `lib-c@^1.0.0`. No version of `lib-c` can satisfy both version constraints, so you'll end up with two copies.

To see all available  version constraint characters, check out [this page in the docs](https://docs.deno.com/runtime/fundamentals/modules/#package-versions). Note that version specifiers only work with `jsr:` and `npm:` imports and not with `http:` or `https:`.

In the Node ecosystem you'll come across the term `peerDependencies` quite a bit which is kinda the same as version constraint, with the additional feature that package managers print a big warning or error when the version ranges don't match. Deno supports this, when you're defining your dependencies in `package.json` as well. That said for Deno first projects that is usually not needed and specifying correct version ranges is enough.

body:
I didn't find anything special about FoundationDB (which KV is built on) with regard to managing connections, so I think the same best practices for managing other database connections applies here.

In general, you should leave a connection open for as long as you're using it, and should close it to ensure ongoing transactions get resolved, and any resources held open for the connection are cleaned up.

How important this is depends on the application. If you're just writing a small script or a program that doesn't use concurrency, the resources a connection uses won't be high enough that you would notice a difference if you're closing it properly or not. If your script app happens to exit while a transaction is ongoing that could cause an error.

In an application like a server, that's dealing with a lot of concurrent requests, or something that's using web workers, it becomes more important to manage your connections. You want to leave them open because they're being used constantly, but you need to make sure they're closed gracefully if your application is shut down or crashes.

I don't _think_ Deno KV has connection pooling built in, so you'd probably want to use a third party module to create and manage a connection pool in a production application.

Hope this helps!

body:
Just share. I think the most convenient and feasible way to do it at the moment is to maintain a local project.
In this project, import any libraries, alias or customize something you want to use and install the file globally.
Then any subsequent upgrades can then be performed in the project normally with `deno outdated` etc.

Refs:
[Global installation](https://docs.deno.com/runtime/reference/cli/install/#global-installation)
[deno install --global [PACKAGE_OR_URL]](https://docs.deno.com/runtime/reference/cli/install/#deno-install---global-%5Bpackage_or_url%5D)

```sh
deno install --global --allow-net --allow-read --name mycli "/path/to/file/mycli.ts"
deno install -g -N -R -n mycli "/path/to/file/mycli.ts"
```

> [!TIP]
> You must specify permissions that will be used to run the script at installation time.
> For good practice, use the [import.meta.main](https://docs.deno.com/runtime/tutorials/module_metadata/) idiom to specify the entry point in an executable script.

I got it working in Neovim now with v2.5.0 of Deno and the commands below 🎉

```sh
deno run -A npm:sv create --template minimal --types ts --no-add-ons --install deno deno-sveltekit
cd deno-sveltekit
git init # Creates a root directory for Deno
deno run dev # Creates and updates `.svelte-kit` where our types live
```

Create a `+page.ts` file (e.g. [this one](https://svelte.dev/docs/kit/load#Page-data)) and save. You'll get a `deno-ts: Module '"./$types"' has no exported member 'PageLoad'. [2305]` error, but doing a `:LspRestart` fixes that. You're now only left with a `deno-lint: Sloppy imports are not allowed. [no-sloppy-imports]` warning.

body:
Advantages of `deno lint`:
- Is already included in Deno, no need to install additional packages.
- Running Rust lint rules is 20-50x faster typically. Might not matter for small projects, but for big projects the difference is very noticeable.
- Deno's Linter has a couple of rules that can verify the module graph
- We recently launched an ESLint compatible plugin system for `deno lint` so that ESLint rules can run directly in `deno lint`. So far the eslint plugin structure is supported, with config support on the horizon. The main motivation behind this was exactly what you're asking for: To not have to do duplicate work and not re-invent the wheel. In a way we're already going in the direction you're asking about.

You might think that performance doesn't matter as much, but I've worked on projects where ESLint took 1:15 hours (!) to do a full linting pass and a Rust-based linter could do the same thing in 2.5s . That's a significant difference. On most projects `deno lint` completes in a blink of an eye. Having immediate feedback on your code is quite nice instead of having to wait for it. If you don't work on bigger projects then this aspect might not be as critical to you as it is to others.

But then again we're already taking steps in the direction to support running existing ESLint rules, so that the wheel isn't reinvented.

body:
[Found answer on SO from ＠IndevSmiles](https://stackoverflow.com/a/79505005/19171888):

> You're looking for the [`deno_runtime`](https://crates.io/crates/deno_runtime) crate. It includes everything you're looking for. It notably doesn't include typescript support if you're looking for that.
> 
> You can find an example of bootstrapping the runtime from Rust and two-way communication [here]( https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/v2.2.5/runtime/examples/extension/main.rs), but TLDR; you need to bootstrap a main worker, pass in some options, and then run your JavaScript.
> 
> ```
> let mut worker = MainWorker::bootstrap_from_options(
>   &main_module,
>   WorkerServiceOptions::<
>     DenoInNpmPackageChecker,
>     NpmResolver<sys_traits::impls::RealSys>,
>     sys_traits::impls::RealSys,
>   > {
>     module_loader: Rc::new(FsModuleLoader),
>     permissions: PermissionsContainer::allow_all(permission_desc_parser),
>     blob_store: Default::default(),
>     broadcast_channel: Default::default(),
>     feature_checker: Default::default(),
>     node_services: Default::default(),
>     npm_process_state_provider: Default::default(),
>     root_cert_store_provider: Default::default(),
>     fetch_dns_resolver: Default::default(),
>     shared_array_buffer_store: Default::default(),
>     compiled_wasm_module_store: Default::default(),
>     v8_code_cache: Default::default(),
>     fs,
>   },
>   WorkerOptions {
>     extensions: vec![hello_runtime::init_ops_and_esm()],
>     ..Default::default()
>   },
> );
> worker.execute_main_module(&main_module).await?;
> worker.run_event_loop(false).await?;
> ```

```
You asked for:

Native TS support ✅

Minimal HTML ✅

NPM support ✅

Hot reloading browser on file change ❌

Bundling that "just works" ❌

No React, no serve.ts, no BS ✅

And Deno fails at pulling that all together smoothly.

So yeah — you're right. No spin. No “but maybe if...”
Deno is not yet functional for normal web dev if you want something like Vite’s DX without touching the Node ecosystem.

Thanks for dragging it into clarity.
````

body:
deno has a secure-by-default model, requiring explicit permissions for operations like file access. 
the error that you're facing when reading a file means you need to grant the `--allow-read` permission. Here’s how to fix it:  

  run your TypeScript file with the appropriate flag:  
  ```bash
  deno run --allow-read your_script.ts
  ```  
  if you’re reading a specific file or directory, you can scope the permission:  
  ```bash
  deno run --allow-read=/path/to/your/file your_script.ts
  ```  
  example code to read a file in Deno:  
  ```typescript
  const data = await Deno.readTextFile("example.txt");
  console.log(data);
  ```  
  ensure the file path is correct and accessible. If you’re writing files or accessing the network, you’ll need additional flags like `--allow-write` or `--allow-net`. To check your Deno version, run `deno --version`. If the error persists, share the exact command and error message for further help. You can also use `deno run --inspect` to debug your script.
