body: There is nothing in the html spec that allows you to retrieve the pixels of an `ImageBitmap` instance. There is an open issue about this missing feature in the spec here https://github.com/whatwg/html/issues/4785
author: marvinhagemeister

body:
Also, how to determine format of `img[Object.getOwnPropertySymbols(img).find(sym=>"[[bitmapData]]"===sym.description)]`?

Bytes per pixel (`img[Object.getOwnPropertySymbols(img).find(sym=>"[[bitmapData]]"===sym.description)].byteLength/(img.width*img.height)`) is not enough.

4 bytes per pixel PNG can be:
- `8 bpc RGBA` in gimp export dialog — `red`, `green`, `blue`, `alpha`
- `16 bpc GRAYA` in gimp export dialog — `value&255`, `value>>8`, `alpha&255`, `alpha>>8`
author: xBZZZZ

body:
As marvin pointed out, createImageBitmap does not expose an API to access the internal data.
Currently, the `[[bitmapData]]` stored in the internal slot is a `Uint8Array` that has already been decoded and is missing pixel information.
The original color information cannot be accurately restored from this data; for example, 8bitRGBA has the same number of bytes per pixel as 16bit grayscale with alpha.
In any case, it is not a good idea to rely on internal slots as they are subject to breaking changes due to specification changes.
author: Hajime-san

body:
> In any case, it is not a good idea to rely on internal slots as they are subject to breaking changes due to specification changes.

I can only find `width` and `height` and `close` in [documetation](
https://docs.deno.com/api/web/~/ImageBitmap).

Does `[[bitmapData]]` exist just to waste RAM?
author: xBZZZZ

body:
> Does [[bitmapData]] exist just to waste RAM?

No, it will use as a source of image rendering like Canvas2D or WebGPU etc.
The `[[bitmapData]]` is just a abstract result of how to store the decoded source in the [spec](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/imagebitmap-and-animations.html#dom-createimagebitmap-dev).
It could different implementation approach about it by JavaScript host, like Deno, Chromium, Gecko or WebKit.
author: Hajime-san

body:
> No, it will use as a source of image rendering like Canvas2D or WebGPU etc.

how do they tell apart `8 bpc RGBA` and `16 bpc GRAYA` pixel formats?
author: xBZZZZ

body:
> how do they tell apart 8 bpc RGBA and 16 bpc GRAYA pixel formats?

In Deno, the pixel information is stored in Rust side but not in `[[bitmapData]]` of JavaScript.
The usage of `[[bitmapData]]` is unit testing [here](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/173f26f391cdb43fb3c46a74365619dd77928623/tests/unit/image_bitmap_test.ts#L19) currently.

body: Yeah, we've been keeping an eye on rolldown for quite a while and are in contact with the team behind it. The reason we went with `esbuild` for now instead is simple: It's been more battle tested. At the time of this writing `rolldown` **is not** widely used in production. We built `deno bundle` in a way that lets us easily swap out the bundler behind it. So once `rolldown` matures more, it's likely that we'll switch to that.

author: Think-M

body:
Hi @Think-M ,

That's a great question about handling long-blocking JavaScript in an embedded deno_core runtime.

To give you the most accurate advice, could you share a bit more about your specific setup?

What kind of "blocked" state are you encountering? Is it a CPU-intensive infinite loop, or is it waiting on a Promise that never resolves (like a hanging network request)?

What does your current Rust code for creating and running the JsRuntime look like? A small snippet would help.

In general, terminating a blocked runtime often involves using the v8::V8::TerminateExecution API from a separate thread, but the exact implementation can vary. For example, if you're dealing with blocking operations on a separate thread, you might need to look at deno_core's resource management.

Once I have a clearer picture of your use case, I can provide a more targeted code example. Looking forward to hearing more details!

body:
No, we're not going to throw away Deno and start from scratch. There are many features in Deno that don't exist in Node.

> I think this new focus is a tragic mistake;

Quite the opposite actually. The Deno user base has seen a drastic uptick since we went strong on Node compatibility. Now people can integrate Deno into their existing projects which they couldn't do before. This makes adoption of Deno a lot easier.

The more people that can use Deno, the better. Even if it means reevaluating past strategies a little. You can find more details in the reasoning in various posts on our blog.

body:
I think that it is not a good idea to add clients for third-party services or software to a language runtime's built-in API.
(Secretly, the same goes for the Deno.Kv API.)
Users lose control over the version of the backend.
And you will have to wait for the language runtime to fix it if a vulnerability is found in the implementation.

In this regard, I don't think `node:sqlite` or C# `System.Data.SQLite` is a good idea either.
It is a good design for web frameworks to transparently support ORMs for DBMSs like PostgreSQL or MySQL, for example, Ruby on Rails' Active Record.
However, it would suddenly seem strange if Ruby supported the PostgreSQL client API.
IMO, the best thing would be to improve development support and compatibility for third-party libraries.

FYI, an issue seemed to be fixed recently that is related to `npm:postgres`.

- https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/28526

body: Deno uses the dotenvy crate which strips quotes from .env values (standard dotenv behavior), while Docker's --env-file preserves them (Docker-specific quirk, see moby/moby#46773). For `KEY="a b c"`: Deno returns `a b c`, Docker returns `"a b c"`. Workarounds: (1) use separate .env files for each tool, (2) normalize in Docker entrypoint: `export KEY=$(echo "$KEY" | sed 's/^"\(.*\)"$/\1/')`, or (3) use docker-compose which handles quotes more consistently.
author: anntnzrb

body:
I’ve noticed the same behavior when using Docker with .env files.
A simple workaround that worked for me was to remove the quotes and handle spaces carefully, like:

KEY=a b c

Alternatively, using a parser that correctly handles quoted values can help.
It’s interesting to see how Deno and Docker handle this differently. Thanks for sharing your findings!

body:
The `denoland/deno:bin-*` images contain glibc-compiled binaries incompatible with Alpine's musl. Either use the official Alpine image `FROM denoland/deno:alpine-2.5.6`, or copy glibc libs from distroless:

```dockerfile
FROM denoland/deno:bin-2.5.6 AS deno-bin
FROM gcr.io/distroless/cc AS cc
FROM alpine:3.19
COPY --from=cc /lib/*-linux-gnu/* /usr/local/lib/
COPY --from=cc /lib/ld-linux-* /lib/
RUN mkdir /lib64 && ln -s /usr/local/lib/ld-linux-* /lib64/
ENV LD_LIBRARY_PATH="/usr/local/lib"
COPY --from=deno-bin /deno /usr/local/bin/deno
```

body: You could try using a simple file exporter in OpenTelemetry. Most OTEL SDKs support exporting traces or metrics to a local file instead of sending them to a full server. For example, in Deno, you could write a small script to collect OTEL data and append it to a file per day. Even a basic implementation that writes JSON lines to a .log file can work. This way, you avoid setting up Grafana but still capture all the data for later analysis.

body: Binary-reproducible vendor directories aren't currently feasible because JSR's meta.json includes ALL available versions at download time, not just the lockfile version. Your suggested workaround is correct: post-process the vendor directory to remove extra version entries from meta.json and point 'latest' only to the used version. Alternatively, use `deno compile` which properly bundles all metadata for offline use.

author: BertramYe

body:
Hey! The issue is with how you're configuring the `script` and `interpreter` fields - they're conflicting with each other.

When you set both `script: "deno"` and `interpreter: "deno"`, PM2 gets confused because it's essentially trying to run `deno deno run start ...` which doesn't work.

Here's what should work:

```js
module.exports = {
  apps: [{
    name: 'blog',
    script: 'npm',
    args: 'run start',
    cwd: './'  ,
    instances: 1,
    exec_mode: 'fork',  // use fork mode, cluster doesn't work well with Deno
    autorestart: true,
    restart_delay: 3000,
    max_restarts: 10,
    watch: false,  // recommend disabling in production
    max_memory_restart: '800M',
    log_date_format: 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss',
    error_file: './logs/error.log',
    out_file: './logs/output.log',
    env: {
      NODE_ENV: 'production',
      PORT: 3000,
      HOST: '0.0.0.0',
      NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED: '1'
    }
  }]
};
```

A few things I changed:

1. **Removed the interpreter stuff** - just let PM2 call `npm run start` directly (or `deno task start` if you have that set up)
2. **Changed to fork mode** - cluster mode doesn't play nice with Deno since it uses Node's cluster module internally
3. **Disabled watch** - generally better for production; you can enable it for dev if needed

Alternatively, if you want to call deno directly:

```js
script: 'deno',
args: 'task start',
interpreter: 'none',
```

The `interpreter: 'none'` is key here - it tells PM2 not to wrap the command with another interpreter.

Also heads up: the `--compat` flag was removed in Deno 2.x, so you don't need that anymore.

Let me know if this works!

body:
@BertramYe You are very close - the key detail is that PM2 must run the correct interpreter/entry command.

For a Next.js app, the most stable production path is usually Node (
ext start) instead of trying to run Next itself with Deno runtime.

If this is a Deno app under PM2, set interpreter and args explicitly (for example deno + un -A server.ts).

References:
- https://docs.deno.com/runtime/manual
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/deploying

body:
Deno recommends using native environment variables via Deno.env.get() and controlling access with the --allow-env permission. For local development, you can use a .env file with --env-file, but in production, environment variables should be injected by the deployment platform (Docker, systemd, CI/CD, or cloud provider), not loaded from files at runtime.
I hope this answer helps you, good luck

body:
I understand what the problem is now.
I added
```toml
winapi = { version = "=0.3.9", features = ["std"]}
```
and now it compiles correctly.

In winapi,
```rust
pub mod ctypes {
#[cfg(feature = "std")]
pub use std::os::raw::c_void;
#[cfg(not(feature = "std"))]
pub enum c_void {}
```
Only when the "std" feature is enabled can it compile correctly.
The deno library doesn't explicitly declare this feature, but it may have been implicitly enabled due to features from some other dependencies.

body:
this looks like expected behavior tbh. `Deno.env.toObject()` needs to read all env vars to build the object, so `--allow-env=PWD` isn't enough — it only grants access to that one var.
If you just need PWD:

`console.log(Deno.env.get("PWD"));`

the blog post is a different story — it clearly shows toObject() working with --allow-env=SOME_SECRET and returning a filtered object. So either that was the actual behavior back in 2.6 and something changed, or the blog post is just wrong. Might be worth opening a separate docs issue for that
author: felipeinf

body:
Building on @felipeinf's answer about the blog post being off: I'm fairly sure the apparent filtering in the 2.6 post is a side effect of how the example is launched, not a real permission narrowing.

That example invokes the script with a shell prefix like `SOME_SECRET=foo deno run ...`. The shell sets that one variable in the child process before `deno` starts, so the *real* process env the script inherits only has that variable plus whatever Deno/libc seed it with. When the script calls `toObject()`, the enumeration returns what looks like a filtered result, but it's actually enumerating the full (very small) environment. Run the same code from a normal shell where `PATH`, `HOME`, `SHELL` etc. are all inherited, and `toObject()` demands broad `--allow-env` again.

So the permission model is consistent: `Deno.env.toObject()` needs unrestricted `--allow-env` because enumeration is itself the side effect. A granular `--allow-env=PWD` never gates toObject, and there's no way for it to, since it'd have to read every var name to know which ones to filter.

For one-off access:

```ts
const pwd = Deno.env.get("PWD"); // works with --allow-env=PWD
```

Worth opening a docs issue on the 2.6 post so the example is clearer about what the shell prefix is doing vs what the permission is doing.
author: everton-dgn

body:
This is a **reported regression / behavior change** in Deno 2.7.x, not user error. The Deno blog post you referenced uses an older permission prompt format; in recent versions, `--allow-env=VAR` grants **read access to VAR specifically**, but `Deno.env.toObject()` is implemented as a bulk read that enumerates **all** environment variables under the hood.

### The technical reason

`Deno.env.toObject()` internally calls `Deno.core.ops.op_env()` (or equivalent), which does an `environ()` system call. This syscall returns the **entire environment block**, not individual variables. The Deno permission system sees this as a request to access the full environment namespace, hence the wide permission prompt.

This is different from `Deno.env.get("PWD")`, which resolves a single variable and the permission system can match it against your `--allow-env=PWD` grant.

### What you're seeing vs. what the blog promised

The blog example from Deno 2.6:
```bash
$ SOME_SECRET=foo deno run --allow-env=SOME_SECRET main.ts
{ SOME_SECRET: "foo" }
```

This worked because `toObject()` was **filtered at the JS layer** in that version: it called the bulk API but then iterated and only exposed the keys matching the permission allowlist. In 2.7.x, the filtering moved or the permission check happens before the JS layer sees data.

### Workaround: iterate the allowlist manually

If you need `toObject()`-like behavior with fine-grained permissions, build the object yourself from your known allowlist:

```typescript
const ALLOWED = ["PWD", "HOME", "USER"] as const;

function envToObject(): Record<string, string> {
  const env: Record<string, string> = {};
  for (const key of ALLOWED) {
    const val = Deno.env.get(key);
    if (val !== undefined) env[key] = val;
  }
  return env;
}

console.log(envToObject());
```

Run with:
```bash
deno run --allow-env=PWD,HOME,USER main.ts
```

This never triggers the wide permission prompt because each `Deno.env.get()` is individually authorized.

### If you really need the full `toObject()` with partial permissions

There isn't a clean way today. Two options:

1. **Grant `--allow-env` broadly** (less secure, but works):
   ```bash
   deno run --allow-env main.ts
   ```

2. **File a Deno issue** — this is a legitimate ergonomic regression. The expectation that `--allow-env=VAR` + `toObject()` returns only `{ VAR: value }` is reasonable, and Deno core team has discussed it before.

### Checking your exact Deno version

Run:
```bash
deno --version
```

If you're on **2.7.12** specifically, this behavior is known. If you're on **2.8+**, check the [Deno changelog](https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases) — there was talk of reintroducing JS-layer filtering for `toObject()`.

### Recommended path

For now, avoid `Deno.env.toObject()` in scripts that run with fine-grained `--allow-env`. Use the explicit allowlist pattern above. It's more maintainable and self-documenting anyway — future readers of your code can see exactly which env vars the module depends on.

If this is blocking a library you're building (where you don't control the CLI flags), consider accepting an env object as a parameter and defaulting to `Deno.env` only in a compatibility layer:

```typescript
export function myLib(options?: { env?: Record<string, string> }) {
  const env = options?.env ?? Deno.env.toObject();
  // ...
}
```

This lets callers pass `envToObject()` or a mock object in tests.

Does the explicit allowlist approach unblock you, or do you need true dynamic enumeration of only-granted variables?

body:
The warning is telling you that the `node_modules` directory was populated for your host platform — packages with platform-specific native binaries (via npm `optionalDependencies`) only contain the binaries for the platform you installed on. When you then `deno compile --target=...` for a different platform, those binaries don't match, which is what's causing your runtime errors.

The fix is to populate `node_modules` for the target platform first, using the `--os` and `--arch` flags added in Deno 2.8:

```bash
deno install --os=linux --arch=arm64
deno compile --target=aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu ...
```

(Substitute your actual target.) That tells the resolver to pretend it's on that platform when fetching `optionalDependencies`, so the right native binaries end up on disk before `deno compile` bundles them in.

More details in the [2.8 release post](https://deno.com/blog/v2.8#cross-platform-npm-installs) and the [docs](https://docs.deno.com/runtime/reference/cli/install/#deno-install---os-and---arch).

Supported `--os` values: `aix`, `android`, `darwin`, `freebsd`, `linux`, `openbsd`, `sunos`, `win32`. Supported `--arch` values: `arm`, `arm64`, `ia32`, `mips`, `mipsel`, `ppc`, `ppc64`, `s390`, `s390x`, `x64`.
